W GAELS,
The following pages are not intended as a detailed
of the Gaelic Athletic Association. Briefly, they

@ record of its growth numerically and financially
ng that period. They will constitute a continuation,
 were, of Mr. T. F. O’Sullivan’s excellent book, ** The

writers on the subject, Mr. Seumas Upton
anf. "), now editor of ‘“Honesty’, whose voice and
we ever been at the service of the Gaels and their
- The splendid material left by my old friend has

I desire to express my thanks to the staff of the
KiLrexxy JoUvrvaL, who were most careful and attentive
during the setting up of this book. Particularly I must
mention Mr. Dick Loughman, foreman, whose unfailing
courtesy and valuable suggestions I highly appreciate.

Briefly, fellow-Gaels, I trust this compilation will be of
use to you. I hope it may prove a silent peace-maker and
arbiter when words are hot and opinions hard as to the date
of some particular mateh, the players who participated, or
the scores registered. Such occasions arise frequently im
Gaelic cireles. Finally, T hope it will be appreciated by the
Gaels of the countryside, as well as by those in the city
clubs and colleges, and I further hope that its perusal wilk
be an incentive to our younger generation to add by their
actions, perhaps, another bright chapter or two to tha
future annals of our great organization.

mra,
P O’NEILL.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

CHAPTER I

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1909

Before procceding with the year mentioned in the sub-
itle, 1 think it well to give a summary of the year 1909
, introduction to my work. The year 1909 was a very
‘remarkable one from a Gaclie standpoint. It was the first
r in which the Championships were brought up-to-date,
also marked the transfer of the Annual Congress from

Tt was remarkable, also, in the fact that

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A. -

gate receipts at matches under the jurisdiction of the Council
came to £509 15s. 7d. in 1908. The Council started the year
with a credit balance of £33 18s. 10d. This was increased
to £100 14s. 6d. during the twelve months.

The gate receipts of the Ulster Council figured out at
£69 19s. 7d. This Council also reported a satisfactory sum
in hand to commence the next year’s work.

Election of Officers,

Alderman Nowlan re-elected President unanimously.
Mr. L. J. O'Toole, again Secretary. Trustees: Messrs. J.
Nowlan and M. Crowe.

Some Motions.

A motion by Dublin County Board to have All-Ireland
medals 10 dwt. 9 carat gold was passed, after a long
discussion. It was also decided that the Croke cups be
cg;:upeﬁed for by the runners-up in the Provincial Champion-.
ships.
A motion that no man hold the office of Chairman of
the G.A.A. for more than two years in succession was
passed by 31 votes to 26,

A motion to hold the Annual Congress on Easter

Sunday was passed by 87 votes to 20. Motions to hold the
‘Annual Cengress in other venues, such as Galway, Cork,
Belfast, ete., were defeated.

A motion by Mr. Crowe to set up a College Champion-
ship for all the Colleges in Ireland was adopted, and the
proposer paid a tribute to the work being done for the native"
games by the Munster Colleges” Committee.

A resolution was carried to the effect that all objections
and applications for tournaments be written on Irish paper.

A strong resolution was adopted, on the motion of
Mr. John Hogan, calling on the Senate of the National
University to make Irish an essential subject for matricula-
tion, and asking the Irish people “‘not to subscribe funds
for any purpose, ecclesiastical or otherwise, in connection.
with the new University unless and until we get satis-
factory guarantees that its curriculum shall be in harmony
with the demands of Irish-Treland.”” There were only two
dissentients.

A vote of thanks was passed to Mr. F. B. Dineen for
This services to the Association, proposed by Mr. Pat McGrath
and seconded by Mr. Dan Fraher.

A Committee was appointed to rewrite and revise the

Rules.
6

‘Social Reunions.
In a leading article in the KILKENNY JOURNAL, January
13th, a strong appeal is made for G.A.A. social reunions to
bring the members together and to diffuse a healthy national
pirit amongst the different units. The Editor reminds his
readers of the various social gatherings organised by Rugby,
‘Hockey, and Tennis Clubs, and asks why the Gael should
ot assert himself more strongly in the social life of iis own
country.

~ In a match organised by Rev. E. Fitzgerald, C.C.,
Cork, the Kilkenny hurlers visited the Leeside City on
Sunday, 2lst February, and gave a thrilling exhibition of
hurling against the Cork selection. There was & erowd of
0,000 people present, and the display was the finest seen
gince those two teams met in the All-Ireland final eight
nths before at Dungarvan. Mr. M. F. Crowe, of Dublin,

Tullaroan v. Mooncoin,

These old rivals met in their tie in the County Cham-
pionship at Deer Park, Carrick-on-Suir, on Sunday, March
14th, 1909, and the game resulted in an overwhelming-
vietory for Mooncoin. Result :—Mooncoin, 6-11 ; Tullaroan,
2 points. The names of the teams will be of interest:—
Mooxcory—J. Fielding (captain), E. Fewer, Ned Doyle, Dick:
Doyle, R. Doherty, J. Ryan, R. Morahan, M. Doyle,
J. Butler, J. Brennan, P. Fielding, R. Walsh,
J. Kelly, 'J. ‘Quinn, E. Hennessy, J. Hennebry,
M. McDonald (goal).

Turraross—Sim Walton (captain), Jer Doheny, Fat Clohosey,
. Grace, J. Dunn, E. Holohan, Dick Grace, Jack
Keoghan, J. Hoyne, P. Dowling, P. Hogin, J.
Grace, J. McCormack, J. Molloy, P. Dowimg, T

Kilkepny Suspended—The Shield Crux.

At a meeting of the Leinster Council held in Dublin on.
St. Patrick’s Day, 1909, the Kilkenuy All-Treland team was
suspended. The matter arose out of a long dispute as to
the ownership of the Railway Shield, which Kilkenny had
won the previous year. The constitution of the team was.
the sole subject of the debates and the dispute continued
for many months. On the other hand, the Munster Council
bhad handed over the Football Shield to Kerry, the majority

of the winning team being Kerry men. In the case of"

Kilkenny, four senior clubs were involved—Mooncoin,
Tullaroan, Erin’s Own, and Threecastles. At a subsequent
meeting of the Central Council, an appeal from Kilkenny-
County Committee was heard, protesting against the action:
of the Leinster Council; and Mr. Dan Fraher proposed,
seconded by Mr. Larry Roche, that the suspension be-
removed. Mr. Dan MecCarthy moved an amendment,
seconded by Mr. Austin Stack, that the decision of the-
Leinster Council be upheld.

The Chairman, Mr. James Nowlan, holding that the
Leinster Council had no power to suspend the clubs, ruled
accordingly, and the matter was thus disposed of.

With regard to the ownership of the Shield itself, the-
matter was. referred back to the Leinster Council. At a
subsequent meeting of the latter hody, the question was-
postponed to a special meeting of the Council. At this.
special meeting, which was held on Sunday, 25th April, it
was decided by 8 votes to 6 to hand over the Shield to-
Kilkenny. Thus the ownership of this famous trophy was-
finally settled

‘The Cork Hurlers in Kilkenny.

The second game in connection with the tourney for
-the Munster Feis was played in Kilkenny on Sunday, May
2nd. The match was well up to the standard usually dis-
played by those two famous teams, but Cork won the
honours on the following scores:—Cork, 4-7; Kilkenny, 1-9.
Mr. M. . Crowe was again referee, and about eight
jtI.’hm]x‘sand spectators witnessed the game at St. James’s
“Park.

-John O'Leary Memorial.

On Sunday, 16th May, a handsome Celtic Cross was
‘unveiled in Glasnevin Cemetery as a memorial to John
O’Leary, the Fenian Chief. Several bands attended, and
many members of the G.A.A., of which the dead patriot
was Patron, marched in procession. The cross was unveiled
by Mr. Neil John O’Boyle, of Antrim, an old comrade of
the Fenian Chief, and no speeches were delivered.

iKilkenny County Final,

The Kilkenny Senior Hurling Final was brought off at
~St. James’s Park on Sunday, 16th May, between Mooncoin
and Threecastles. The Southern men had the best of
“matters right through the game and emerged victors on the
“following score : —Mooncoin, 5-7; Threecastles, 8-5. Mr.
Jer Doheny was referee.  This was Mooncoin’s fourth
“successive final.

“Thurles v. Dungourney.

In a challenge contest at Thurles, May 30, the Thurles
Blues, All-Treland Champions, defeated Dungourney on the
“following scores:—Thurles, 3-9; Dungourncy, 2-8. Mr.
Mick Maher, of Tubberadora, was referee.

"Dr. Douglas Hyde and the G.A.A.
Speaking to the Gaels of Limerick cn Sunday, 6th

June, Dr. Douglas Hyde, then President of the Gaelic

“Neague, stated, during the course of his address:—

“T always thought that one of the influences that
actunted Michael Cusack was the feeling that when
Tie was promoting Irich games, he was promoting
the spirit of Irish Nationality; and I may say that,
second to the Gaelic League itself, the work of the
Gaelic Athletic Association in keeping alive that
most vital and important, feature in the life of the
Nation—its_sports, its pastimes—has tended more
than anything else to keep alive the fact that we
are in Irelmh\g and part of Ireland.”

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

Tribute to Matt Gargan.

““Carbery”, writing a series of articles in “The Cork
S)‘)ortsms.n" on ‘“Hurlers I Have Met”, pays an eloquent
ribute to Matt Gargan, the elusive Kilkenny mid-field man.

“*Carbery” writes :—* Of good medium stature, square and.

very bony, with powerful shoulders and sturdy legs
inclined to bandiness, the Kilkenny mid-field wonder is
a hurler every inch of him. No spare flesh here—bone:
and sinew alone contribute to his twelve stone and a
half. His face is the face of an athlete too—strong,
firmly set, square of jaw and keen of eye. This is the
outward aspect of the flashing, brilliant, long-pucking
Matt Gargan of Kilkenny's All-Treland seventeen.
¢ - He is the personification of concentrated vitality
—always moving, always watchful as a cat. Thmuqf:
the smallest break, he makes an opening and then the-
goal-keeper can look out. Somewhat erratic, he is a

most dangerous man to let loose at any stage of a-

contest.”

Cork v, Kilkenny.

In the third of the series of matches between Cork and
Kilkenny for the Munster Feis Medals, played at Dungarvan.
on Sunday, 22nd August, the game resulted in a draw
atter one, of the finest exhibitions ever given on Fraher's.
field. The teams were practically the same as played on

the two former occasions. The scores were 2 goals 9 points-

each. Mr. Tom Kerwick (Thurles) was referee.

The Munster Final,

A great game for the Munster Scnior title between
Cork (Dungourney) and Tipperary (Thurles), in Tipperary
Town on August 29th, ended in a win for the Premier-
County by four points. Tipperary, 2-10; Cork, 2-6.
:*Vigilant”, describing the game, states:—*This is a game
dndeed that might rank with the contests of the Fiamna.'”
The gate receipts were £160 6s. 9d.

0'Meagher-Condon in Kilkenny.

On_Saturday night, 2ud October, amid scenes of un-
bounded enthusiasm, Captain O’Meagher-Condon received
the Freedom of the City of Kilkenny. He was met at the
station by a huge crowd accompanied bv bands and a torch-
light procession escorted him to the Town Hall. Several
addresses were presented from the various local bodies. The

12

addresses and welcome so spontancously given the lFem‘sn
“veteran were due mainly to his active participation in *‘the
smashing of the van” at Manchester and his heroic conduct
“in the dock when sentence of death was passed upon him.
~ He it was who originated the immortal prayer of “‘God Save
- Ireland.”

were £62 10s. 7d.

The All-Ireland Hurling Final,

The All-Treland Hurling Final for.1909 was played in the-
Cork Athletic Grounds between Kilkenny and Tipperary.
Great interest was evinced in the contest, and, although in
the depth of winter, thousands poured into the Leeside City
to witness one of the greatest games ever played for the All-
Ireland crown. At half time Kilkenny led by 3-4 to 8 points.
In the second half, although the game was stubbornly con-
tested at every angle, Kilkenny won well on the following.
scores :—XKilkenny, 4.6; Tipperary, 12 points. Mr. M. F.
Crowe was referee, and the teams were :—

Kiugessy—Dick Walsh (“Drug”), captain ; Eddie Doyle, Dick

Deherty, Jim Kelly, Dick Doyle, Joe_ Delahunty,
Jim Ryan, Mick Doyle, Bill Hennebry, Jim Dunph;
(goal) (Mooncoin): Matt Gargan, Mick Shortall,
Paddy Lanigan (Erin’s Own); Jack Keoghan, Sim-
‘Walton, Dan Kennedy (Tullaroan); Jack Rochford
(Threecastles). Sub.—Dick Grace (Tullaroan).

Trererary—Tom Semple (capt.), T. Kerwick, Martin O’Brien,

Hugh Shelly, A Carew, Paddy 0'Brien, Paddy
Burke, Jack Moohey, Joe McLoughney, J. Mockler,
“Hawk”® O'Brien (gbal) (Thurles); Jim Burke and’
Jack Hackett (Two-Mile-Borris) ; Jack and Fat Fitz—
gerald (Glencoote) ; Jim Gleeson (Holy Cross); Bob-
Mockler (Horse and Jockey). ¥

Some Opinions of the Game.

Two writers to the “‘Cork Sportsman’'—*‘Carbery”’ and.
*‘Kinalea’'—gave their opinions of the game as follows:—

In the course of a long description, ““Carbery’’ states: —
“ It was wonderful to see how accurate Kilkenny were
under the troublesome conditions; they showed s
command over the leather that one rarely sees om
such a sod. . . . It was not a brilliant game—
the ground decided that ; but it was a good final never--
theless—strong and honest all the way, containing"
shining passages, though slow and crude at times.
That grand effort of Tipperary in the second half was,
to my mind, the most striking feature—it was really
wonderful to see the recovery they made. At one

14

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

time it looked as if they would mow down Kilkenny
like grass before a scythe. But in combination and
scoring ability they were far behind.”

“Kinalea" says:—" It was not, perhaps, of that great
swiftness and brilliancy which might be expected in
an All-Ireland in which Kilkenny was figuring. Tt
was powerful hurling just the same—all the time high-

class, and parts of it were impressive. The chief

points that struck us were—the deadly aceuracy of
the Kilkenny scorers, the wild shots of the Tipperary
orwards, the magnificent wing play of Gleeson and
of Doyle, the cool and confident defence of Dunphy in
the Kilkenny goal, the grand blocking and returning.
of Burke, the clearing of Rochford, the speed of
Shelly, the centering of Lanigan, the lifting of Ken-
nedy, the accuracy of Delahunty, the tactics of Ryan,
the penetrability of “Hawk’, and the failure of
~Semple. . . . The crowd was a tremendous one.
~ Tt cheered and surged with enthusiasm. It was an
ter-provincial tie, but no narrow clannish spirit
linded our sense of National pride. We rejoice in
> game as the apotheosis of Irish skill and Trish.
- muscle.”

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

(CHAPTER 1I)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1910.

The year 1910 marked another great ehapter in the pro-
-gress of native games, particularly in the Schools and
*Colleges. In the hurling final two teams, Limerick and
Wexiord, were pitted, that had long been out of the running
in the provincial championships. In the beginning of the
year many rumours were afloat that a hurling and football
team of leading players would be sent to the States during
the summer, but the project never materialised. During a
holiday at home, in the summer of 1909, Father J. K.
TFielding, of Mooncoin and Chicago, interested himself in
the idea and endeavoured to work up the tour, promising
hearty support and co-operation at the other end, but the
project never materialised.

The series of matches organised for the Munster Feis
between Kilkenny and Cork was a great success and drew
immense crowds. As a result, hurling became immensely
popular and the game was taken up enthusiastically in
quarters where it hitherto received very little support.

The amicable settlement of the Shield question between
Kilkenny and the Leinster Council was a source of satisfac-
tion to all true Gaels, although the dispute prevented Kil-

“kenny from entering the lists for the 1908°championship.
It was during the year 1910 also that the Junior inter-

The Belgian Tour,

Through the initiative of Mr. J. J. Walsh, Chairman,
Cork County Board, a hurling tour was organised in connee
tion with the session of the Pan-Celtic Congress at the
Brussels Exhibition in the beginning of September. A
team from Cork and Tipperary was selected to give exhibi-
tion matches in Belgium—one in Brussels, another at
Malines, and the third on the historic plain of Fontenoy.
'l’l}a idea of the tour was a splendid one, but it failed
miserably as a means of popularising the game on the Con-
tinent. The _nrranlg‘ements at the Belgian end were faulty
snd.wl.mh might Lave been an excellent opportunity of
putting Ireland’s case for self-determination before Europe
failed flatly.

The teams left Treland on Friday night, 26th August,

and played their first match on Sunday in fhe Belgian
capital.  The teams, led by a piper, marched from #he hotel
in their playing togs and made a favourable, though amusing
impression on the populace. Tipperary won two of the
matches and Cork the odd ome. On arriving home the
hurlers got a great reception and bands preceded an immense
procession in Cork to the City Hall, where the hurlers wera
formally welecomed home and several speeches delivered.
The tour was a big financial failure and some time later the
late Mr. Bigger, of Belfast, gave a lecture in the Cork Cit;
Hall to help to wipe out the debt. As Mr. Bigger aptly
said of the tour: “‘Ireland was on the parade ground of
Turope and failed to march past.”” F

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.

(CHAPTER III)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1911.

The year 1911 marked another big advance in the
history of our National Athletic body. The new rules were
generally received with favour, though there was a good
deal of adverse comment expressed on the limitation of the
scoring area. Many old hurlers contended that some
features of the old game, such as ““whip” play, would be
lost, but conceded that the reduced scoring area would
.eventually develop more accurate ball play and more con-
-certed forward work. The introduction of the square, or
parallelogram, too, was a decided improvement and gave
‘the goalkeeper a chance of delivering the ball. Previous to
this change, it was not unusual to see the full forward and
the goalkeeper having a friendly chat together while the
ball was down the field. Another commendable innovation
was the system of having the backs in their places before
the ball was thrown in. This prevented speedy forwards
“from making a rapid score before the defenders were in
‘position. The introduction of nets behind the goal-posts
‘had also a good effect in preventing disputes as to scores.

The championships lapsed this year again and were
not played off until 1912, when Cork won their second All-
Treland Football Championship against Antrim in February.
The hurling final proved ebortive, Limerick refusing to play
Kilkenny in Thurles, after the fixture had to be e lradilr
in Cork owing to the inclemency of the weather. The
Munster Council then nominated Tipperary to represent
the province, and they were beaten by Kilkenny, who were
awarded the All-Treland medals. The games made good
progress in the schools and colleges, and the formation of &
Loinster Colleges’ Committee put the games on a'solid
foundation in the Eastern Province.

It was only at the beginning of this year that Jones's
Road, now Croke Park, became a purely Gaelic freld.

The Annual Convention.

Railway Shield Presented.

On Monday night, 6th February, in the Town Hall,
Kilkenny, Mr. Dan O’'Connell, County Secretary, on behalf
of the G.A.A., presented the beautiful Railway Shield won
by Kilkenny to the Kilkenny Corporation. Mr. O Connell
gave a history of tha Shield, which, briefly, will not be out
of place here. .The £l -was put up for competition by
.the” Great Southern Railway Company fn 1905 for com-

petition amongst the four provinces, with the right of selec-

match against the pick of Munster at Carrick-on-Suir on
the 20th August, 1905. They later defeated Galway in
Limerick on the 11th Novembeér the same year. Tipperary
won in 1906 at Dublin, but Kilkenny were victorious in
‘September, 1907, when they defeated their old opponents,
o Tipperary. The final contest took place at Kilkenny on the

perary, 2-5. Rey. Father Dollard (**Slievenamon") threw

burlers in the Shield and All-Ireland competitions.

No Loyal Address!

A large and enthusiastic meeting of Waterford citizens
was held i the Town Hall on Sunday, 21st May, to protest
against the presentation of loyal addresses to the King and
Queen of England on the oceasion of their visit to Ireland.
Mr. Owen Dawson presided and there were many local
representative men on the platform.  Stirring addresses
were delivered by Mr. Maurice Quinlan, T.C.; Dr. McCar-
tan, The O'Rahifly, and Miss Helena Maloney; and letters
were read from Major John McBride, Dublin, and Mr. Sean
Etchingham, Wexford. The resolution of protest was passed
with acclamation.

Cuffe Shield. :

The final of the third round was played at Jones’s Road.
on Sunday, 9th April, between Tipperary and Wexford. It
was a hard, close game for forty minutes, and close marking
and “‘chopping’’ were much in evidence. Wexford led at
half time by one point, but the typical Tipperary dash was.
responsible for the Premier County’s strong win. Tipperary
7-2, Wexford 2-1. Mr. Kennefick, Dublin Co. Board, was
referce, and the gate receipts were £230.

Cork v. Kilkenny (Munster Ties Tourney).

The fourth game in connection with the Munster Feis
medals, between Cork and Kilkenny, took place on Sunday,
28th May, at Cork, and resulted again in a draw. The first
match played at Cork in February 1909 was a draw; the
second at Kilkenny resulted in a win for Cork; the third and
fourth, played respectively at Dungarvan and Cork, also
ended in drawn games. Sunday’s result:—Cork, 1-4; Kil-
kenny, 2-1. Mr. M. F. Crowe was again referee.

At Fermoy, Sunday, 11th June, Waterford and Clare
drew in senior hurling—1-1 each; and Waterford junior
footballers disposed of Clare—Waterford, 8-3; Clare, 1 pt.

Kilkenny v. Wexford.
At Wexford Annual Feis held at New Ross, June 4th,
Kilkenny defeated Wexford (senior hurling), 2-2 to 1-1.

On the following Sunday the same two counties met-

at Waterford in the Leinster Championship, when Kilkenny
had a dual vietory. Hurling: Kilkenny, 4-1; Wexford, 8-0;
Football: Kilkenny 8 goals; Wexford, 1 point. It was the
first important gathering at the Waterford venue since it
has been made a real Gaelic field.

Death of Captain J. 1. Laracy.

The death occurred, at the end of June, of Captain J.
J. Laracy, late of the Transvaal Irish Brigade, a nave of
Kilkenny. He fought under Colonel Blake and Major
John MecBride through the Boer War, and received his
commission from President Kruger. Taken prisoner, he
was sent with the Boer prisoners to Ceylon, where he con-
tracted the disease from which he died. There was a large
attendance at the funeral. R.LP.

A Chip of the Old Block.
At a big athletic meeting in Fermoy on Sunday, 2nd

July, Mr. Maurice Fraher, a son of Mr. Dan Fraher, of’

Dungarvan, and only a youth of eighteen, created much
surprise by covering 20 feet 8 inches in the long jump and
defeating such well-known athletes as Paddy Roche of
Bandon and O’Leary of Copeen. Mr. Fraher is now owner

of Fraher Field, Dungarvan, and worthily upholds the Gaelic

traditions of his late respected father.

James Stephens’ Memorial.

A representafive Convention of bodies interested in the
above project was held on Sunday, 20th October, in the
Town Hall, Kilkenny, Mr. G. Stallard, Mayor, presiding.
Many of the hurling and football clubs of the county were
represented.

An eloquent tribute was paid to the Fenian Chief by
Mr. Donleavy, who stressed the benefits that accrusd to
Treland as a result of the Fenian Movement.  Thanks,™
he said, “ to the Fenian men, Treland is lifting herself from
the dust, drying her tears, and proudly demanding her
legitimate place amongst the nations of the earth. Through
the pages of Irish history the name of James Stephens will
be handed down as a hallowed memory like that of Emmet
and Fitzgerald to inspire men with high ideals of patriotism
and devotion.”

Mr. E. T. Keane, * Kilkenny People,” ably proposed
the resolution that the Stephens’ Memorial was worthy of
the support of all Irishmen, particularly the men of Kil-
kenny at home and in exile.

Mr. Doheny, seconding, said they had gathered that
day to admire and respect the unselfishness, patriotism,
loyalty, and high ideals which inspired James Stephens and
his companions of the I'enian Movement.

Kilkenny lost badly to Antrim in the semi-final of the
All-Treland Football Championship, played at Jones’s Road

half time—1-1 to 1-0; but in the second period fell away
hopelessly. As “‘Vigilant”” expressed it: ‘“They went to
Pieces back and forward; they played recklessly and gave
away free after free.” Final scores:—Antrim, 3-1; Kil-

kenny, 1.1.

Death of Captain Cuife.

| The death occurred towards the end of the year, at
- Freemantle, Australia, of the Hon. Otway Cuffe, a member
of an old Kilkenny family. He had been closely in touch
with the various Irish-Treland movements and was a
generous benefactor to every ceuse with which he was.
associated. For the Ring College at Dungarvan he gave a
magnificent Shield for competition amongst the leading
- hurling counties and was deeply interested in the industrial’
~revival. He made a very commendable effort to revive:
~ knee breeches, woollen stockings and shoes as a suitable

ess for Irish boys. Votes of sympathy were passei by
numerous public bodies on the passing of this remarkable.
hman, who, though not of the people’s class or creed,
ped them generously in their various movements. R.LP.

(CHAPTER IV)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1912

not a happy one for the National
‘Athletic Association—at least for the governing body. An
unfortunate dispute concerning the venue of the All-Treland
Hurling Final between Kilkenny and Limerick created a
situation that, for a time at least, threatened to undermine
the G.A.A. 1 shall briefly recapitulate the details.
The All-Ireland Hurling Final between Kilkenny and Lime-
rick was fixed for the Cork Athletic Grounds on the 18th
February. The day came terribly wet, the pitch was &
uagmire, and it was utterly impossible to carry out the
xture. The match was postponed, although the Limerick
team appeared on the field, and, as a sequel to this gesture,
. claimed the match. The matter came up before a sub-
sequent meeting of the Central Council, who refixed the
match for Thurles by a vote of 4 to nil, on the 21st April,
- with Mr. Tom Irwin, of Cork, as referee. Limerick objec
and brought the matter to the Annual Congress, held in
Dublin at Easter, and the decision of the Central Council
~was upheld. Limerick, however, remained adamant and
their slogan was ““ Cork or nowhere.”” To put the matter
briefly, Limerick refused to play in any other venue but
Cork’ and were subsequently suspended. To cover the
- expenses incurred by the Central Council for the debacle at
Cork and to redeem the prestige of the G.A.A. in the eyes
of the public, the Munster Council was asked to nominate
a team on behalf of the Province, and Tipperary—Munster
“finalists against Limerick—mantully stepped into the breach.
The match was played on the last Sunday of July at Dun-
garvan and after a strenuous game Kilkenny emerged
Victors en the scores—8-3 to 2-1. Thus Kilkenny vindicated
their claim and were recorded as All-Treland Champions for
1911.

‘Decided at Last!

The final of the scries of matches on behalf of the

garvan. The tourney took three years and a half to finish

vietorious, and the third and fourth in Dungarvan and Cork
respectively, when two other draws resulted. TIn the match

enny mustered thirteen.

sal was up to the highest standard evar displayed between

magnificent display in the second half and it speaks volumes
for both teams when it is recorded that no score was made
in the second period. Several competent judges of hurling

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A,

(CHAPTER V)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1913

The year 1918 marked another bright milestone in the
progress of the G.A.A. Increased finances, increased crowds
and consequent increased popularity made the G.A'A. one
of the strongest national institutions in the country. Speak-
ing in present-day parlance, the National Athletic Associa-
tion had “‘turned the corner” and its position now in Irish
life was one from which its most bitter opponents with all
their Imperial artillery could not dislodge it. The games of
the Gael, both hurling and football, had created an interest
amongst the plain people that stirred them to their hearts’
depths, and that attracted not alone them, but the higher
classes of Irish life. The latter minority saw that the
G.A.A. had come to stay. We had condueted our games
with dignity and decorum ; they were played with the spirit
of true sportsmanship, and, better still, to their-mind. wo
had provided them with a healthy feast of friendly rivalry
and chivalric antagonism .

Thus I briefly record our doings in 1913. The Croke
Memorial competitions enlisted our best hurling and football
ents and thousands of disinterested spectators came to see
what Gaelic football was like when Kerry and Louth gave
~ their marvellous display on the 4th of May. Briefly, “those
who came to mock, remained to play,”” and ever since then
Gaelic foootball, played by any two wel own teams, will
command as much
Shelbourne Park or Lansdowne Road.
The reduction in the number of players from seventeen
fteen made the game more strenuous and more active
for the participants and enlivened the game to a much
greater extent than we had ever imagined. This year also
saw the first Junior All-Treland Championships played, when
Cork won their junior title in hurling, and Tipperary after
most exciting display against Louth notehed their names
Treland’s first All-Ireland Junior Champion footballers.
_ The year 1918 marked a big step forward in the G.A. 5
2 it is with pleasure I record the principal events of our
‘ational Athletic Association durimg that period.

entral Council.

January, at Dubiln, with Alderman Nowlan in the chair.
E 65

patronage as any “international”’ at .

. The quarterly meeting of above was held on Sunday,

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.AA.

The Secretary’s report on the finances of the Councit
was eminently satisfactory. The receipts on all matches
for 1912 under the supervision of the Council amounted to
£1,451. They had a loss on some matches that were held,
but after paying all expenses they had a net gain of £829.
The Council had also a sum of £308 put aside for the r

ed memorial to the late ‘Most Rev. Dr. Croke, Archbishop
of Cashel, who was one of the first patrons of the Associa-
tion.
Tt was decided, aiter considerable discussion, to invest
700 in Great Southern and Midland Railway stock. )

Croxe MemoriaL.—Fixtures were then made in aid of
the Croke Memorial Fund. Hurling: Clare v. Qalw_:i
Dublin v. Wexford; Kilkenny v. Teix; Cork v. Limeri
Tipperary to. play winners of Clare v. Galway mateh.
Football: Cork v. Kerry; Dubin v. Louth. The Council
decided that the winners in both departments should receive
gold medals.

by. Mr. M. F. Crowe, seconded by Mr. J. McCarthy, that
Munster and Leinster Junior Champions play A“v[r_elgnd
Finals. Any player who took part in semior county, inter-

from March 1972 to February 1913 came to £886 10s. 3d.;

nearly £100 to credit. )

Maher (Tipperary), and Mr. J. Murphy (Waterford).

OtueEr ImporTANT MaTTERS.—The motion to have all
All-Treland Finals played in Dublin was defeated by 40
votes to 83. Provincial Councils were empowered to grade
any county under its jurisdiction either senior or junior, or
have either junior or senior hurling or football inter-county
teams.

The Central Council was directed to take shares in the
Belfast Gaelic Grounds.

Very Rev. Canon Sexton proposed, and Mr. D. Rurke

(Dublin) seconded :—*“ That this Convention ecalls on the
outstanding Colleges of Ireland to give preference to the
pastimes of their own country.”—Passed unanimously.
P. D. Mehigan proposed and Mr. Harry Boland
That a distinctive county colour be compulsory
for inter-county, inter-provincial, and All-Treland contests,
such colours to be approved by the Provincial Councils con-
cerned and registered with the Central Council.”'—Passed.

On the proposition of Mr. Harry Boland (Dublin)
seconded by Mr. J. Lalor, the numbers of players was
reduced to fifteen, this rule to come into force on and from

. 1st May.

Tre FoorsaLL RepLay.—The replay of the football final
for the Croke Memorial was the greatest gathering ever
seen up to that period at Jones's Road. Upwards of fifty
thousand were present and the gate receipts amounted to
£1,183. The game between the old rivals, Kerry and
Louth, was a classic exhibition of Gaelic football in which
the finer features of the game were displayed to advantage
in spite of the sweltering heat. The crowd was enormous
and thousands had to be refused admission. At half time
the teams were even as on the former occasion—Kerry, 1
goal; Louth, 3 points. The last half was stubbornly fought
out, but the staying power of the Kerry men was the
deciding factor and, they emerged worthy winners on the
following scores:—Kerry, 2-4; Louth, 5 points. Mr. Jack
MeCathy, of Cork, was referee, and the teams were :
Kenry—Dick Fitzgerald (capt.), D. Doyle, P. Breem, J.
Skiiner,” Gony. Muidhy, P, Hedy (il

Moriarity (Listowel).

The Croke Memorial.

At the quarterly meeting of the Central Council held on
Sunday, 6th July, in Dublin, Alderman Nowlan, President,
in the chair, the question of the Croke Memorial was dis-
cussed. The tournament was finished on June 20th, and
the receipts from all matches amounted to £2,735 8s. 4id.
and the expenses were £862 17s. 1d. That left a balance on
hands of £1,872 6s. 83d., which with the palance on hands
for the Memorial prior to the tournament of £493 3s. 4d.
made a total of £2,365 11s. T3d.

£100 was voted to the Secretary, Mr. L. J. O'Toole,
as a bonus out of the tournament.

After discussion, it was decided to erect a monument in
Thurles to the great prelate and patron of the G.A.A., and
also to purchase a grounds with the residue of the money.

At a later meeting £300 was voted to Thurles Con-
fraternity Hall.

Munster Championship.

Cork and Limerick played the semi-final of the Munster
Championship at Thurles on July 13th before an enormous:
crowd. After a hard, close game Cork won. Scores:—
Cork, 8-3; Limerick, 1-2. Mr. Tom Semple was referee.

Big Camogie Match in Cork.

The Kilkenny colleens travelled to Cork on Sunday,
20th July, to engage the Leeside ladies in the first inter-
county contest of camogie. The display was a first-class
one and a large crowd witnessed the encounter. The scores
were :—Cork, 2-2; Kilkenny, 1 goal. Mr. M. Mehigan was
referee and the receipts were £60. That night the Kilkenny
;ﬁlsyers were entertained to a ceilidh at the Gaelic League

- Rooms.

a leading member of the Fenian Brotherhood and brother

the Fenian prisoners from West Australia.

Death of Patrick Forde,

... The death is announced of Mr. Patrick Forde, of the

Trish World"" (New York). He, with Michael Davitt and
Matt Harris, was mainly responsible for the spread of broad
and deep ideas as to the solution of the land question in
Ire]and: His paper, “ The Irish World,”" was the medium
of sending £8,000,000 to Treland to help the people during
the Plan of Campaign. R.LP.

TY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

Annual Congress.

._The Annual Congress of Gaeldom was held in the C;
Hall, Dublin, on Easter Sunday, 9th April, Alderman
Nowlan, President, in the chair. The proceedings were of a
lengthy nature and many rdforms were discussed and
carried. The financial statement submitted constituted a
record. The auditors’ report stated that the total cash
received from March 13th, 1913, to March 19th, 1914, was
£4,486 1s, 4d., of which £4,391 1s. 11d. was from gate
receipts. Of this figure of £4,391 1s. 11d., £1,656 11s. 104d,
represented gate receipts in connection with twelve Central
Council fixtures, and £2,784 10s. 10}d. gate receipts in
connection with six matches for Croke Memorial. As a
*‘gate” the culminating figure was £1,198 taken at Jones's
Road on June 29th, 1913, for the replay of Kerry and Louth
match. The total disbursements amounted to £4,279 10s.
13d., and the balance in bank on March 19th, 1914, was
‘£381 18s. 3d.

The dispute concerning the Croke Memorial was amic-
ably settled, the Central Council to give an additional £200,
as well as the Munster Council an extra £200, so as to bring
the sum up to £1,000.

A proposal to form the Association into a limited
Tiability company was passed, and left to a sub-committee
consisting of Messrs. Hogan; Crowe, and O'Toole.

Purchase of Jones's Road.

Mr. J. B. Dineen’s interest in Jones’s Road grounds
was purchased on the 22nd December, 1913, and became
the property of the G.A.A., to be known henceforth as
Croke Park. The purchase money, including law costs,
ete., amounted to £3,641 8s. 5d. £1,641 8s. 5d. of this
was paid in cash and a bill given to the bank for £2,000.
Championships.

The following resolution was passed:—'* That future
All-Treland Senior Championship Final be played in Croke
Memorial Park on fixed Sundays in each year, one on the
first Sunday in September, and the other on the 4th Sunday

 in September.”’

~ Also passed:—** That winners of provincial and inter-
~ provincial junior championships cannot again take part for
two years in these competitions.”

tion willingly agreed to give another £200 if the Central

On the proposition of Mr. J. J. Keane, seconded by

threatened to create a very bitter split between Tipperary
and the Central Council was averted by the above-named
gentlemen.

The claims of both parties were most commendable,
and the Central Council can be acquitted of unfair dealin,
when it is remembered that Jones's Road (the venue of al
Gaelic matches in Dublin) was then in the market and that
they were anxious to secure it as a practical and permanent
memorial to the first patron of the Association. Both sides
were satisfied. Tipperary got the £1,000 demanded, as
well as £300 extra towards the renovation of the Thurles
Confraternity Hall, whilst the Central Council were enabled
to pay their first instalment—£1,500—towards the purchase
of Jones’s Road, grounds and lodge a bill in the bank for
the remaining £2,000, the full purchase money being
£3,500.

The foundation stone of the Croke Monument was laid
in Thurles on St. Patrick’s Day, 1920, amid a scene of
popular rejoicing, and Jones’s Road was secured for all
time as the Croke Memorial Park, wherein no other pastime
but the games of the Gael be played. Thus closed a chapter
of Gaelic history that at one period threatened to disrupt
the relations between Tipperary and the Central Council,
but which terminated happily in the best interests of the
Association.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

CHAPTER VII

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1915

The year 1915 was not an extremely remarkable one in
G.AA. annals. It is pleasing to state, however, that the
splendid position that the Association gained during the pre.
ceding three years was well maintained in every respect.
Both All-Treland Finals went to Leinster—Leix winning
the hurling from Cork and Wexford triumphing over Kerry
in football. Tt was Leix’s first Blue Riband.

The general attendances at the bigger games were well
up to standard, and the football final brought a record crowd
to Croke Park. Money, too, was voted by the Councils
concerned for the training cf their provincial champions for
the finals, and this was the first occasion on which such a
procedure was officially followed. The war had naturally
an effect on the Association, and it is sad to relate that a
few one-time prominent members of the G.A.A. turned
recruiting sergeants for England and made a special appeal
to “‘the hurlers and footballers of Munster.” The Associa-
tion generally, however, stood firm to its old ideals and to
the principles of its founders. An event that must not be

omitted in this summary was the funeral procession and
burial of the Fenian veferan, O’Donovan Rossa, early in
August. Tt awakened an old and tender chord in the hearts
of Irishmen and sent a thrill through the country that was
only equalled by the martyrdom of Allen, Larkin, and
O’Brien in 'Sixty-seven.

Wolfe Tone Memorial Matches,

The matches in aid of the Wolfe Tone Memorial during
the year were well contested and patronised and the games
helped to give training and experience to many counties that
had succumbed in the championships.

Cork County Convention,

At the Cork Annual Convention, Mr. J. J. Walsh, who
had been deported to England some months previously, was
re-elected Chairman. There was a deficit of £84 on the
year's working, mostly due to the abandonment of the
~county hurling final between Midleton and Blackrock, and
to_the occupation of the Athletic Grounds by the British
military authorities. E

Croke Cup Football Final,

Played at Croke Park, before a fairly large crowd, on
“Sunday, 24th February. Cork, 2-1; Louth (holders), 3
points. Thus Cork won the cup in hurling and football.

Death of Jack Grace.

The death is announced of Mr. Jack Grace, captain of
the Dublin Kickhams, and a member of a well-known Kil-
kenny family. He was brother of Dr. P. Grace, Porl
Laoighise, and of Mr. Dick Grace, Tullaroan, well-known
All-Treland hurlers. Both in hurling and football Jack Grace
took part in nine All-Irelands. R.L.P.

Clare, 6-2; Galway, 2-1 (hurling).” Mayo, 8-1; Kerry, 2-0
(football). ~ Receipts, £50.

Munster Convention,

Held at Limerick, Tth March. The following officers
were re-elected :—Chairman, Mr. Jerry O’Brien (Clare):
Secretary, Mr. Pat McGrath. Delegates to Central Council
—Messrs. M. Maher (Tipperary), J. Kelly (Limerick), and
Jim Harrington (Cork). The following motion was passed :
*“ That we recommend for the consideration of the Annual
Congress the advisability of having the constitution and
rules of the Association printed in Trish as well as English."””

The income for the period was £2,270 0. 9d., and the
expenditure £2,256 7s. 1d. The assets as shown by the
balance sheet total £6,015 185, 5d., and the liabilities £3,673
11s. 8d., or a balance to credit of £2,342 6s. 9d. The
liabilities include £1,000 in respect of the Dr. Croke
Memorial as decided at the last Convention.

The secretary (Mr, L. J. O'Toole) stated in his report
that the company was duly incorporated under the Com-
panies Act, 1908 and 1913. Improvements have been
effected at Croke Park—£978 for erection of concrete wall
on the enclosure side of the grounds, and £115 in an addi-
tion to existing wall on the G.S, and W. Railway. Side line
seats capable of accommodating 1,500 people were procured
at a cost of £115. The erection of banking was proceeding
satisfactorily.

CroRE Cup FiNaLs.—The 1914 Croke Cup competitions
in hurling and football realised £115 Ss. 6d., whilst the
expenses in connection with same were £180 odd. The
loss would have been considerably more but for the fact
that the matches were played in Croke Park and that no
rent charges were made.

receipts totalled £1,805 14s. 2d. The Secretary thauked

Cork Championship.

Midleton defeated Carrigtwohill in the Cork Senior-
Hurling Championship in the Corl Athletic Grounds, May
16th—DMidleton, 4-1; Carrigtwohill, 8 goals. Mr. Tom Trwin.
was referee.

A Rival Organisation,

For some years past the Kickham Club of Dublin had:
differences with the Central Council, and decided on form-
ing a new organisation, entitled ** The National Association.
of Gaelic Athletic Clubs.”” The seceders got a little support
in Wexford, where they organised a tourney with the co-
operation of the local Gaels, but after some time the new
organisation fizzled out and the Kickhams came back to-
their old allegiance.

Writing on the matter, “‘ Vigilant "' stated :—** The.
G.A.A. stands for the same ideals as it did on the day of its
inception. Tt is because it stands for ideals that are near-
and dear to the hearts of every true son and daughter of
our Motherland that it is an object of suspicion by every
foe and renegade.”

Leinster Ties.

Sunday, 23rd May, Kildare defeated Wicklow in senior
and Meath in junior football. Meath won the junior hurling.
tie from Kilkenny.

Dublin Championships,

Played at Dublin, 80th May, Geraldines defeated Par-
nells, 7 points to 1 point in the senior football final, and
Faughs defeated Co]ll;;iaus in hurling—6-2 to 8-2. There
was a big crowd present and some of the matches were
played in the evening, starting at 6.30 p-m.

Leix Defeat Kilkenny in Hurling,

Leix sprung another surprise this year on Kilkenny by
defeating them at Tullamore in the semi-final of the Senior-
Leinster Championship. The scores at half time were :—.
Leix, 81 to nil for Kilkenny. The Black and Ambers made
a great rally in the second half, but failed to overhaul their
opponents, who played a masterful game right through the
hour. Full time:—Leix, 4-1; Kilkenny, 2.6.

“* Vigilant,”” writing of Kilkenny’s wonderful recovery,

says:—** Never did men fight against such a stern irony of

did these fifteen boys in black and amber.

In the Leinster Championships played at New Ross on
Sunday, July 11th, Dublin defeated Wexford in senior hurl-
ing and Kilkenny defeated the Metropolitans in the junior
-event. Final scores (senior hurling) : Dublin, 4-8; Wexford,

P. Dunphy, of Abbeyleix, was referee.

A Gruelling Munster Final.

One of the hardest finals ever played for the Munster
title took place at Thurles on Sunday, 18th July, when Cork
and Limerick entered the arena. Interest in the match was
widespread and gartisan feeling was at fever pitch. There
- was a crowd of 20,000 people present. Much open betting

was indulged in, which was mainly responsible for an un-
- happy and untimely ending to what was a dour and deter-
‘mined clash. In the first period no score was recorded at
eithier side, which showed the closeness of the struggle.
The Cork men asserted themselves well in the second half
and looked likely winners, when the match was abruptly

ips. “ There was not anything remarkable in the way of
science in the play, but it was a hard, fast, and clever dis-_
play, in which it was ‘man-to-man all the time'" (“Vigilant").
Cork were disallowed two goals in the first period, and the
same fate befell a major for Limerick in the second period.

©’Donovan Rossa.

The remains of this celebrated Fenian veteran were lnid
to rest in Glasnevin on Sunday, 1st August, amid a striking
manifestation of militant national faith. Volunteers, some
of them fully armed, came from all parts of the country to
pay their lact tribute to this noble Trishman and to renew
their vows of loyalty at his grave.

The procession from the City Hall, where the rema‘ns
had been lying in state, was one of the most remarkable
demonstrations that ever graced the streets of Dublin. The
late Padraig Pearse gave the oration over the grave of the
dead patriot, and his tribute still remains as one of the most
remarkable pronouncements ever rendered by one gallant
Trishman towards another. Pearse spoke as ‘‘one of the
younger generation who had been re-baptised in the Fenian
faith, and who had accepbes‘ the responsibility of carrying

out the Fenian programme. i . i
. Tn the course of his brilliant and touching oration.

Pearse said:—'* O’Donovan Rossa was splendid in the:
proud manhood of him, splendid in the heroic grace of him,.

The Hurling Blue Riband.

Leix won their first All-Treland hurling final on
October 24th, when, in precence of g huge crowd, they
defeated a much-fancied Cork combination. The game was
well up to final standard, although heavy rain fell in the
second period, which marred the display to some extent and
made it difficult for the players to keep their feet and their
hurleys. At half time Cork led by the minimum margin—
3 goals to 2-2. In the second period Leix completely out-
-played the Cork men and showed befter dash, opportunism,
and combination. Final scores :—Leix, 6-2; (Cork, 4-1.
Mr. Willie Walsh, Waterford, was referee, and the teams
were :—

Lerx—J. Finlay (capt.), J. Walsh, T. Finlay, Jim Carroll,
Jack Carroll, Joe Carroll, J.' Daly, B, Campion. J.
Phelan, J. Hiney, John 'Phelan, E. McEvoy, Bob
O’Keeffe, J. Dunphy, P. Ryan’(goal).
Corx—Connie Shechan, “Bowler” Walsh, Larry laherty,
Willie Fitzgerald, Sean Hydo, J. Ramsell Mick
Byrne, P, ’ﬁuckley, “Mayor” Kennedy, T. O'Riors
dan, P. Halloran, Tim Nagle, Sean Og Murphy, J.
Murphy, Barry Murphy (Cloughdus) (goal).

** Vigilant,”” writing of the game, says:—‘ I have wit-
nessed a good many hurling finals and I can truthfully say
that I never saw one that was played out from start to stop
in a finer or more admirable sporting spirit. There was no
denying the fact that Cork was confronted with the biggest
and most amazing surprise that has ever been effected in
the Guelic athletic arena. There was no luck in it, and
beyond all possible shadow of doubt the better team won,
and won conclusively and in the most decisive manner
imaginable."”

Seventeen ‘‘specials’” ran into Dublin for the game and
14,000 people attended.

General Eoin O'Duify.

Writing of the prominent personages he met at the
match, “Vigilant” says:—“At the Thomond Hotel, the
“headquarters of the Leix team, the first man that met me
at the door was down from ‘the Black North’, but his
welcome was as open-hearted and warm as Tramore sun-
shine. Eoin 0'Duffy comes from Clones ; he has a Northern |
accent and the Northern manner, but he is a Gael to his
Enger-tips and a Nationalist to the core.’

“Vigilant™ also pays kindly reference to my friends,

Pat Lee and Mike Doyle of Waterford.

The death is announced at this time (December 1915)
of Captain O'Meagher-Condon, who was sentenced to death
in company with Allen, Larkin, and O’Brien for his partici-

death of Sergeant Brett at Manchester in 1867,

ENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

(CHAPTER VIIT)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1916

The year 1916 was not alone a memorable one for the
G.A.A.; it was a glorious and memorable one for the Irish
Nation.  After a lapse of forty-nine years “the faithful few "’
asserted the right of their country’s independence with
arms in their hands. There is no need in a compilation like
this to enlarge on the heroic men and the noble principles
that guided and urged them on in a futile but glorious
attempt to assert Ireland’s individuality. Their effort was
timely. Burope was plunged into a ghastly and bloody war
on the pretence of freedom for small nations, and a self-
sacrificing band availed of the slogan to bring Ireland’s case
before the world. How well they succeeded abler pens than
mine can portray. Our National Athletic Association nobly
contributed its quota of heroic men, who left aside their
camans for more deadly weapons. Later in the internment
camps of Frongoch and Ballykinlar the games of the Gael
were practised assiduously, and the healthy chivalric spiril
of our hurlers and footballers permeated the foetid atmos-
phere of British dens and dungeons. The idenls of ‘the
founders of the G.A.A. were carried to their logical con-
clusion,

As a consequence of the Rising and its aftermath, the
natural progress of our pastimes was temporarily stopped,
but after a time they flourished again with greater vim and
vitality. The spirit of the Gael was not broken, and the
Munster Final at Dungarvan saw a mighty hosting of Irish
‘manhood and Trish womanhood that was sufficient answer to
those who thought that Trish Nationality was dead for ever.
In Leinster, too, the championship ties were well attended,
end after a month or two the Gaelic Athletic Association

ursued its normal course. Wexford won the football title
ab the expense of Mayo, and “‘Gallant Tipperary,” repre-
ented by the sturdy boys of Boherlahan, notched its name
again on the national hurling escutcheon. The G.A.A. has
0 reason to be ashamed to speak of Easter Week, and when
te history of that period is being written the rank and file
f Treland’s greatest sporting organization must not be
rgotten.
This year, too, Irish-Treland lost two sterling defenders
 her faith in the persons of the Rev. Doctors Hennebry

| Michael O’Hickey, pioneers of the Language Movement.

‘Death of Frank B. Dineen.

The death is recorded at this period—April 1916—of
Mr. F. B. Dineen, a famous athlete and one-time President
and Secretary of the G.A.A. Born in Ballylanders, County
Limerick, he showed athletic ability at an early age, and
took part in the first big athletic meeting organised by the
.G.AA. in Bandon in 1885. He was first President of the
G AA. Athletic Council, and compiled the ““Irish Athletic
Record” in 1906. He purchased Jones's Road grounds
1908, and in 1913 sold them to the G.A.A. for £8,000, since
‘when they became known as Croke Park. In the early days
-of the Association he took sides with the Physical Force
Party against the Constitutionalists, and, earlier, was
imprisoned as a “suspeet’” under “‘Buckshot' Foster’s
regime. R.IP.

Tribute to Dr. Henebry.

At the Easter quarterly meeting of the Committee of
“Management of Ring Trish College, the Very Rev. Michael
Sheehan paid an eloquent tribute to the late Dr. Henebry,
who had been interred a few weeks before. In the course
of his address Dr, Sheehan said: ‘“He died at a time when
the seed he sowed was whitening for the harvest. He saw
‘the young people whom he helped to train through the
medium of Irish at Ring, winning scholarships at University
“College, Cork. His eyes glistened with triumph when he
perceived that they had caught from him the sacred fire,
“and he looked forward to the day when they could bear their
‘willing part _in helping to realise his National Ideal—his
ideal of an Treland loyal to her past, cultivating her dis-
tinetive music and art, speaking her own language and
employing it as_the medium of higher education. . . -
We do not stand apart. We are one in grief, and we know

The Annual Congress.

The Annual Congress of the G.A.A. was held in i
on Easter Sunday, April 28rd. As a report of the pr%t‘:gi
ings is not available, owing to the Rising, we give par-
ticulars of the balance sheet and statement of accounts.
The receipts of the Central Council for the year ended
February 20th, exclusive of loans, were £1,782°18s. 7d., a
decrease of £887. The revenue account of the Council
-shows a profit on the year's working of £166 6s. 5d., and

The Easter Rising.

Tt is not within my pr
this to give a detailed account of the E:
and elsewhere. Suffice to say that the spirit of the men of
1916 became the general spirit of Treland six months later,
though many of our public representatives at the time fel¥
over each other in their anxiety to pass resolutions *‘con-
demning the cut-throats and renegades that stabbed England
in the back when she was holding out the hand of friend-
ghip”’! The old Jegend that “England’s diffieulty is Ire-
Jand’s opportunity”” was pigeon-holed for the time, and the
teachings of Tone, Mitchel, and Davis were for otten. Time
and tradition have vindicated the men of ““Sixteen’’ and
their names are honoured to-day wherever a Gael has found
a home,

The effect of the Rising on the G.AA. was easy to
understand. Many had deserted the playing-piteh for the
drill-ground, and it was not surprising that the bulk of the
clubs should eventually suffer. Many prominent Gaels were:
amongst those imprisoned or interned, and Kerry withdrew
from all active participation in the championships as a
result.

«Enniscorthy’s in Flames.”

A band of Irish Volunteers entered Enniscorthy on.
Thursday morning, 27th April, and took possession of the
town. ‘‘As a precautionary measure, and with a view to
defending the town from attack, a very large number of
citizens, including the Mayor and the ex-Mayor of Wexford,
the Wexford National Volunteers, and as many others as
were required, were sworn in as Special Constables and are
working in conjunction with the RI.C.”

The Last Victim.

Sir Roger Casement, who had been prominently iden-
tified with the Volunteer and Irish-Treland movements
generally, was executed at Pentonville Prison on August
4th. He was received into the Catholic Church some time
g-cix‘gausly, and died a thorough Christian and Irishman.

A Tribute to Two Great Gaelic Counties.

Written by an anonymous Tipperary Gael, the follow-
ing paragraph appears in a long article on Tipperary's
Guelic_activity :—

“‘No two  counties have answered the call of the Gaolic
trumpet so universally as Cork and Tipperary have done, and
the forward position, ‘the broad and national influence, and the.

increasing popularity of the G.A.A. to-day is due in'no small
share to the keen and manly rivairy that has always existed
between Cork and Tipperary on the Gaelic field. Tipperary’s
champion hurlers can always admit the style and skill of Black-
Tock, the Finbarrs, or Dungourney; whilst Cork can tall of s,
/ speed and dash and the science of Thurles, Borris, and Tubber-
adora, the Jockey, and Toomevara,’

So never let old Treland fear

hat none are left her Cause to steer.
For should prond Freedom call again,
For gleaming lines of fighting men,
The first to lead the noble work—
The men of Tipp. and Rebel Cork |

The Munster Crown.

A typical battle for the Munster crown was fought at
Dungarvan on the 1st October, when Tipperary defeated
their old rivals, Cork. The game was well up to the
standard of those two famous counties. ‘‘Never was there
such wonderful hurling sustained right on from start to
stop with increasing interest under such cruelly inauspicious
climatic conditions. As a test of endurance the contest
‘Wwas most exactng, and nothing short of muscle, wind, and
speed perfected to their utmost capacity, by sound and
Systematic training, could prevail under such circum-
tances.” Half time :—Tipperary, 2 goals; Cork, 2 points.
Full time:—Tipperary, 5 goals; Cork; 1-2. There was an
enormous crowd present, and the teams were :—

Writing of the mighty hosting at Dungarvan,
“Vigilant"” says:—"* The enthusiasm of the Gael was writ
large over this fine gathering of faithful and fearless Irish-
men and Irishwomen, who are living in continuity with the
great past of our race. Brother Gaels, this old race of ours
and this old land of ours have seen many changes and will
see more, but whilst there is a God ruling the spheres, the
day will never come when the soul of a Nation can be
strangled or when the unquenchable aspiration of a people
can be finally smothered in a blood-bath.”

Annual Convention, .

The Annual Convention of the G.A.A. was held it

Dublin on Sunday, 8th i 1
Alderman James Nowlan. The proceedings were private,
‘but a curtailed report was later given to the Press. The
‘Becretary’s report and statement of accounts were adopted
after a short discussion. The following delegates attended
from the Central and Athletic Councils:—Messts. J. <«

‘kenny, Wicklow, Wexford, Leix, and Kildare.

On the motion of the County Dublin Committee, it was
decided to request each County Board to endeavour to
organise a Boys’ Sports meeting during the year, and it
was decided to authorise the County Dublin Board to hold
an All-Ireland Sports meeting.

The Secretary of the Central Council submitted a
acheme in connection with County, Provincial, and All-
Treland Schools and Colleges’ Competitions, which was
ordered to be forwarded to each County Board ,with in-
structions to take immediate action.

Kilkenny Feis Match. q
Tipperary had a substantial win—9-2 to 1-4—over Kil-
kenny in a match at the Kilkenny Feis on Sunday, 24th
June. The teams were practically the same as contesf
the All-Treland title in January.

Kerry Not Competing.

At a meeting of the Munster Council notification was
received from Kerry stating that they were not competing
in this year’s championship.

Munster Championship.

Played at Limerick on Sunday, 8th July, Limerick
defeated Cork in senior hurling—5-8 to 7.1, In junior foot-
Dall Cork defeated Limerick—1-1 to nil; and Clare defeated
Waterford in senior hurling—Clare, 6-4; Waterford, 8-2.

The hurling mateh between Cork and Limerick was &
very exciting contest and was one of the fastest and most
scientifie expositions of hurling witnessed in Limerick for a
long time. In senior football Clare defeated Waterford, at
Waterfori—2-6 to 3 points—on Sunday, 15th July.

Clare Defeat Waterford in Football.

Tn the first round of the Munster Semior Championship
Clare defeated Waterford at the Sports Field, Waterford,
on Sunday, 15th July—Clare, 2-6; Waterford, 3 point. The
Clare team were accorded a great reception on their arrival
at Waterford on Saturday evening, being met by the local
Sinn Fein Club and the Fianna Pipers’ Band.

Last Tribute to Tom Ashe.

When the remains of Thomas Ashe were consigned to
their last resting place in Glasnevin on Sunday, 81st Sep-
tember, and when a party of Volunteers had fired threo
volleys over the grave, Commandant Michael Collins came.
forward and said :—"* Nothing additional remains to be said.
That volley which we have just heard is the only speech
which it is proper to make abovs the grave of a dead
Fenian.””

A Voice from the Gaeltacht!

‘“The members of the Old Purish National and Red-
mondite Band are requested to attend at the local band-
roomn, Oid Parish (Ring) on all Wednesdays and Saturdays
between the hours of 7 and 9 p.m. TInstruction will be given
in drums, fifes, cymbals, castanets, and triangle. The
members of this Band are requested to practise regularly
and untiringly in order that their Band may be ready to go
to Dublin and join their patriotic countrymen in the national
celebration which is to take place in the Trish Metropolis on
the opening of our Home Rule Parliament in College Green
in the course of a few months, please God! This brave
Band was established in 1886 and has withstood the storms
of 81 years, strenuously fighting in the cause of unity and
freedom, and wherever the Old Parish Band goes all croakers
and factionists must give way before its irresistible and
mighty onset:

““ Its songs were made for the pure and free,

They shall never sound in Sinn Feinery.
‘“ Up, Redmond! Up, Unity! Up, Home Rule!

‘“ (Signed) ———————— Bandmaster.”
Comment would spoil this precicus gem from the Gael-
tacht.—Suiasn Ruapm.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

(CHAPTER X)
THE ASSOCIATION IN 1918

The year 1918 was not an outstanding one in G.A.A.
-sonals.  The political situation, the conscription menace,
and the “flu”’ epidemic were all contributory causes to the
detriment of native pastimes. In many cases 1t was impos-
sible to carry out the championships, with the result that the
Provincial ties were delayed and the Association, in con-
sequence, suffered. Both All-Treland finals were carried into
‘the following year, when Wexford won their fourth sucees-
sive football final against Tipperary, but succumbed in the
hurling final against their old opponents of eight years back
—Limerick. The ““flu”’ made a heavy toll on our hurlers
and footballers and many promising careers in the arena
were cut short in the bloom of manhood.

Great Athlete Dead.

The death is now recorded (May) of Martin Sheridan,
:a native of Mayo, and three times all-round champion of
the world. _Standing over six feet in height, he was &
‘gplendiil specimen of an athlete and remained true to the
.old principle of Irish Nationality. RIP.

Dublin, All-Treland champions, defeated Kilkenny in

Gaelic Sunday. '
Guaelic games on an unprecedented scale took place all

over Treland on Sunday, August 4th, as a protest against
the recent military regulations, and crowds gave their
support to the fixtures in unstinted fashion. Only in a few
cases did the police mterfere, and the result was a singular
vietory for the G.A.A. E

Roughly one thousand matches were played, which
were intended to challenge the strength of the forces of
Miny and order” in the country at this period.

(aelic Sunday in Belfast Jail.

The Gaels interred in Belfast Jail celebrated Gaelic
Sunday by an inter-provincial football mateh, when Munster
and Ulster combined opposed Leinster and Connacht. The
mateh ended in a draw. Several well-known Gaelic players:
participated and the proceedings were marked with great

enthusiasm.

(CHAPTER XI)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1919

The year 1919 was a very normal one in Gaelic affairs.
It was remarkable, however, in the fact that four All
Ireland Finals were played this year, those of 1918 being
carried over until the beginning of this season. In the
regular finals for the year, Cor , after a lapse of sixteen
years, had a smashing victory over Dublin in hurling, whilst
Kildare easily annexed the " football honours b; dgefen(.in
Galway. In'the Leinster Final they narrowly defeate
Dublin by a point. The standard of the games was well
maintained, public patronage was generously bestowed, and
the financial condition’of the Association generally showed
an all-round improvement. The receipts of the Central
Council, £4,838, were the biggest vet on record.

Towards the end of the year a tournament amongst all
the leading hurling and football counties was started to help
the Croke Park improvement fund and to wipe off the debt
incurred by same.

All-Ireland Football Semi-final (1918),

The above game was played at Croke Park on Sunday,
12th January, between Tipperary and Mayo, when the
former achieved victory by the minimum margin—Tip-
perary, 2-2; Mayo, 1-4. ‘‘The game was splendidly con-
tested throughout, both teams playing strenuously. ~ With
a little luck the Westerners would probably have retired
winners. '

Death of Pierce McCann,

The death is nmow recorded (March) of Mr. Pierce
le:Cn.rm, T.D. for East Tippemrg'. in ()}Ioucsster, after
being removed from the local jail. His death caused a
great shock to all in Tipperary and in national circles
in Ireland. His remains were brought home to his native
Tipperary and his funeral to Ballagh was one of the greatest
popular demonstrations ever seen within the Premier
County. R.LP.

Kilkenny County Convention,

The Kilkenny County Convention was held in the City
Hall on Sunday, 23rd February, with Mr. Sean Gibbons in
the chair. There was a large attendance of delegates pre-
sent. There were three names put forward for the Pre-
sidency—Messrs. Sean Gibbons, Mick Doyle, and Jim
Aylward. The two latter withdrew their names, and Mr.
Sean Gibbons was returned unanimously.

For Vice-President, Rev. Dr. Doyle, C.C., Tullaroan,
was unanimously chosen. Mr. Mick Moore was unani-
mously selected as Secretary; and a vote of thanks was
passed to Mr. Jim Aylward, who filled the post at a critical
period. Messrs. M. Doyle and Dan Kennedy were elected
as representatives on the Leinster Council on a poll, and
Alderman Nowlan and Messrs. Dan O'Connell and Mick
Moore( Secretary) were appointed Trustees. There was a
debit balance of £28.

‘A Night in Tullaroan."”

After the Kilkenny County Convention the Mooncoin
delegates—Messrs. M. Doyle, Pat Walsh, and Jim Carroll—
as well as Mr. Phil O'Neill were invited to a dance -at
Tullaroan by the local Gaels. A very pleasant night was
spent. “It was a thoroughly Trish night, reflective of the
kindly spirit of the Gaels, with the height of hospitality, the
cream of conviviality, and the core of cordiality thrown in.”

Toomevara and Boherlahan met in the Tipperary final
on Sunday, 23rd March, when Toomevara were winners—6

‘ goals to 3-4.

The Annual Congress,

The Annual Congress was held in the Mansion House,
Dublin, on Easter Sunday, 20th April, with Alderman Jas.
Nowlan, President, in the chair.

Mr, Harry Boland announced that the dispute between
the Dublin County Board and the Kickham Club was amic-
ably settled, and the statement was received with satisfac-
tion.

AupiTor’s Report.—The receipts for the vear were
£2,266 5s. 1d., and the disburscments £1,615 3s. 1d. Gate
receipts from four fixtures were £1,402 9s. 9d., or £482 in
excess of the gate receipts from a like number in the pre-
-ceding year. The disbursements were £127 higher than in
‘the previous year; rent and wages showed an increase of
£62, and teams’ expenses £140, but there was a reduction
of £87 in the printing account.

CRORE PARK.—The receipts were £831 14s. 4d., and
disbursements €587 16s. 4d. Ground and pavilion rents
received totalled £770, an increase of £300 on the previous
year's figures. On the other side, rates showed an increase
of £47.
Revexve.—The income for the year was £825 Gs. 4d.,
and the expenditure (including a reserve of £100 for depre-
ciation as usual), £600 10s. 6d. A sum of £500 was ear-
arked for the Croke Memorial. The balance to the credit
f the Council at the end of the year was £2,437 19s. As g

ult of matches for the benefit of the Kerry County Board
£277 was handed over. A proposal of Mr, Harry Boland’s
at the offices of President, Vice-President, and Treasurer
hould not be held by the same person for more than two
ears was defeated.

It was unanimously decided that Most Rev. Dr. MeRory
e made a patron of the Association.

“Waterford Hurling Final,

Played at Waterford on Sunday, 18th April, Clonea
defeated Ferrybank—4-3 to 2-1—in the 1918 county senior
hurling final.” In the junior match Ferrybank defeated
Clonea by two points,

The Annual Congress,

The Annual Congress was held in the Mansion House,
Dublin, on Easter Sunday, 20th April, with Alderman Jas.
Nowlan, President, in the chair.

Mr, Harry Boland announced that the dispute between
the Dublin County Board and the Kickham Club was amic-
ably settled, and the statement was received with satisfac-
tion.

AupiTor’s Report.—The receipts for the vear were
£2,266 5s. 1d., and the disburscments £1,615 3s. 1d. Gate
receipts from four fixtures were £1,402 9s. 9d., or £482 in
excess of the gate receipts from a like number in the pre-
-ceding year. The disbursements were £127 higher than in
‘the previous year; rent and wages showed an increase of
£62, and teams’ expenses £140, but there was a reduction
of £87 in the printing account.

CRORE PARK.—The receipts were £831 14s. 4d., and
disbursements €587 16s. 4d. Ground and pavilion rents
received totalled £770, an increase of £300 on the previous
year's figures. On the other side, rates showed an increase
of £47.
Revexve.—The income for the year was £825 Gs. 4d.,
and the expenditure (including a reserve of £100 for depre-
ciation as usual), £600 10s. 6d. A sum of £500 was ear-
arked for the Croke Memorial. The balance to the credit
f the Council at the end of the year was £2,437 19s. As g

ult of matches for the benefit of the Kerry County Board
£277 was handed over. A proposal of Mr, Harry Boland’s
at the offices of President, Vice-President, and Treasurer
hould not be held by the same person for more than two
ears was defeated.

It was unanimously decided that Most Rev. Dr. MeRory
e made a patron of the Association.

The Football Final. .
The football final between Kildare and Galway was
played at Croke Park on Sunday, 29th September, when

Banquet to All-Ireland Hurling Champions.

On Saturday night, October 18th, a banquet was given
to the Cork All-Treland Champion hurlers in the Tmperial
Hotel, Cork. The chair was occupied by Mr. Sean Me-
Carthy and the proceedings were marked by great enthu-
siasm. i
Replying to the toast of “‘Our Native Land,” Mr. Liams
de Roiste said never was hope so high, because never e
fore, probably, was the national spirit of Ireland so strong
or the determination to win so great.

Other toasts were:—** The Gaelic Athletic Associaion,
“ The All-Treland Hurling Champions,” The Selection
Commitee,” and ** Our Absent Friends,” referring in par-
ticular to Mr. J. J. Walsh, T.D.; Alderman James Nowlan,
and Mr. Austin Stack, T.D.

Kerry and Kildare.

Tn n benefit mateh for Paddy Dillon, the old Kerry
custodian, who lost his arm in a railway aceident, Kildare
beat Kerry—1-2 to 8 points—at Croke ‘Park on Sunday, 26th
October, before 5,000 spectators.

Arthur Griffith gave his views on the situation to an
interviewer thus:— )

“ The English Government in Treland has now proclaimed the
whola Irish Nation as it formerly proclaimed the Catholic Church
an illegal assembly. There are mo further limits to which its
ridiculous proclamations can soar. The Irish Nation will continue
% live when its proclaimers shall have passed from the memory of
mankind.”

Tribute to ‘‘Carbery.”

In the KILKENNY JOURNAL of September 13th a tribute is.
paid to ‘“Carbery” (Mr. Paddy Mehigan) by ‘‘Sean
Gaedheal.” Inter alia he states:—

4 Ho has ever been a keen worker in Irish-Ireland. A fluent
speaker of Trish, bo leads tho way for others in the ‘movement. A
i ing tho interests of the G.A.A.

“ Carbery,” who is an old and intimate friend of the
writer, comes from Ardfield, Clonakilty, Co. Cork. He
played with the Tondon-Irish in the All-Treland Final of
1902, and later played with Cork. As a writer on Gaelic
topies he has few equals in Treland, and his broadcasting of
Gaelic games has further enhanced his name.

Waterford Football Final.

A splendidly contested match on Sunday, 7th Docer-
ber, between Kilrossanty and Ballymacaw (Gaultier) for
the final of the Waterford Championship, ended m a win
for Kilrossanty on the scores : —XKilrossanty, 3-4; Bullyma-
caw, 1 point. _The winning team was :—Pat Keating (xoal),
M. Ryan, P. Kirwin, J. Kirwin, T. Ryan, Pat Fitzgerald, b
Guiry, P. Lawlor, J. Lawlor, John Power, M. Walsh, M.
Norris, W. Power, T. Kennedy, W. Gough.

Pat Keating, who captained Kilrossanty team, was an
outstanding figure in County Waterford during the Anglo-
Trish struggle, and was killed in the Burgery ambush i
Dungarvan in February, 1921. A splendid monument mar
his grave, as well as that of his brother, who was killed i
the Civil War in 1928, in their native Kilrossanty. R.LP.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

(CHAPTER XII)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1920

The year 1020 can scarcely be written down as a yeur
of Gaelic activity; the people were concerned withi more
serious subjects, rather than the playing of games. ‘Bands-
of dissolute ruffians, financed by an alien Government, were
let Joose on the people, and their track was marked by
murdér, rapine, arson, and assassination. Ireland was in
the apex of her agony. Every day brought news of un-
paralleled outrages committed on our peaceful countryside
by unserupulous armed bands who spared neither priest,
youth, woman, or child. No wonder that our young. men:
thought of other things than hurling, football, or athletic
sports. Many of them were dragged from their beds and
suffered cruel deaths at the hands of the ““Tans” and
Auxilaries.

Searcely had the mortar of the Croke Memorial founda-
tion-stone been dry, when a shriek of horror rang through
Treland at the brutal murder of Tomés McCurtin, the first
Republican Lord Mayor of Cork.. This was followed by
the arrest of Terence McSweeney, who nobly stepped into
the breach and openly declared to his enemies that he
would be a free man within a few months. Next came
“ Bloody Sunday,” when a peaceful crowd of Gaelic spec-
tators were shot down without mercy at Croke Park, and
when #he soil of our National Stadium was made sacred by
the biood of innocent Irish victims, amongst them a seion
of a great Tipperary stock—>Mike Hogan of Grangemockler.

The men of Munster—the backbone of our great

National Athletic Association—threw aside their camans to-

hand®e their rifies and to deal in more deadly confliet with
the enemies of their race apd country. How well they
succeeded history can tell. The hurling and football cham-
pionships were abandoned, and a lapse of two years occurred.
before men were found to engage in the homely, healthy
rivajry for the All-Treland finals, When the 1920 chawmpion-

ships were resumed in 1922, Cork again defended their title

against Dublin, to be beaten by 6-4 to 2-4 by the Dublin.
Colleginns, and Tipperary had a glorious victory over the
Dublm ('Toole’s in the final for the Blue Riband sn faptball.
