TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.
Father Fielding on the Situation.

During this summer Father J. K. Fielding of Chicago,
a native of Mooncoin, and a well-known worker in the Irish.
Ireland movement in the States, paid a visit to Treland and
gave his impresssions of the conditions and political outlook
then prevailing in this country.

** The Irish people,” he said, ** need have no doubt as
to the feeling of every right-thinking man and woman in the
States, irrespective of creed or nationality, respecting the
present situation in Ireland. It is not a question of craving
concessions under the name of Home Rule or Land Acts
now, but a clearly defined issue of smashing the connection
that has been the curse of this country for centuries.

* Ireland is the oldest of the small nations for which
America wants the blessing of democracy. Ireland’s fight
for freedom extends over 700 years; this fight has been
renewed in every generation down to the present. The
boundary line of the Irish nation is clearly defined; God
Himself took care of that. Ireland is better able to main-
tain her independence than any of the newly-formed Repub-
lics. Treland has as much right to self-determination as
any of the other small nations.”

Death of Pat Corcoran.

One of the Old Guard of Kilkenny Nationalists passed
away on Tuesday, 17th August, when Mr. Pat Corcoran’s
death was announced. ““Suffice to say that in that grave at
Foulkstown lies a man whose life was the impersonation of
true Chirstianity and uncompromising patriotsm, a clear
and serious thinker, who never lacked courage to say or do
the thing in which he believed.” R.IP.

Death of Terence McSweeney.

After a hunger-strike lasting several months Terence
MeSweeney, the heroic Republican Lord Mayor of Cork,
passed away in Brixton Prison at 5.40 p.m. on Monday
morning, 25th October.

*“ In death he exemplifies to mankind the faith that the
:pirit' of the Irish Nation is indestructible and unconquer-
able.”

** Put back your shoulders when you hear his name,

And lift your head ; your race is honoured move
Than any man has honoured it before."’

meeting of the Central Council was held on Tuesday, 26th

it Provincial Councils, County Boards, Leaguc, and

ournamont Gommittees are Hereby requested to make ne:
Sunday, 81st October, a closed date all over Treland so_as
B e sympathy of the Gaelic Athletic Association with _the
Ty Mayoress of Cork in her great bereavement, out admiration
for the Lord Mayor's heroic sacrifice, and to mark the Associa-
tion’s protest against the inhuman treatment meted out to the
Tat6 Lard Mayor of Cork by the British Government.””

“Bloody Sunday.”
©On Sunday morning, 21st November, twelve leading
members of the British Secret Service were killed in Dublin.
Most of them were British military officers who had been
sent to Dublin to counter the activitics of the Volunteers.
Tn the evening, whilst a challenge football mateh between
Tipperary and Dublin was in progress at Croke Park, lorry
1omfs of Auxiliaries and military appeared on the scene and
fired volley after volley on the inoffensive crowd. The
scene was indeseribable as the thousands present sought
cvery means to escape from the deadly fusilade. The
pleasant picture was changed into o bloody pandemonium.
‘Twelve people were killed outright, whilst several were
seriously wounded. One of the Tipperary players. Michael
Hogan, was killed and presented a pathetic spectacle as he
lay on the sod in his football togs. The Hogan Stand at
.Croke Park has been named in honour of this heroic martyr

to the eause of native games. RIP.

(CHAPTER XIII)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1921

The year 1921 was another quiet period in G.A.A.
aunals.  For the first six months of the year the dreadful
agony of Ireland continued, and all rejoiced in early July
when a truce was declared
the Irish Republican forces. It ws
history that Perfidious Albion had called a truce with *'the
mere Irish,” and everywhere there was unrestricted out-
bursts of joy and rejoicing. Honest, unoffending citizens
walked abroad again to enjoy in unmolested security the
natural life that God ordained to them and to live again in
the security and sanetity of their own homes,

Immediately after the decluration of the truce, Irish-
Ireland activity became general and the Irish colleges were
thronged with earnest and. enthusiastic students.- The
Leinster Hurling Final,. plaved on Sunday, 21st September,
between Dublin and Kilkenny, marked the opening of
Gaelic activity, when Mr. Michael Collins addressed the
players and started the game.  Associated on the occasion
with him were many of the prominent leaders, who had
of coming out amongst their own people

} h that Treland—Gaelic Treland—had seen the end
of her long night of agony. Corlk, true to its rebel name
and tradition, decided that the fime had not yet arrived to
take up native pastimes, and exhorted her young men to
continue to devote their energies to the cause of national

devyation to Treland by execution in Mounjoy Jail on Monday
morning, the 24th April. His case had excited more than
ordinary sympathy and a subseription list for his widow and
rphans was initiated by the late Mr, James O'Neill of
insale with a subscription of £5. He died in perfect re-
ignation to the tenets of the Catholic Church and true to
e highest tradition of uncompromising Fenianism. R.I.P.

A Famous Final Recalled.

Rev. J. B. Dollard, the poet-priest of the Guaels, gives
a bright and vivacious ‘account of the All-Treland Final of’
1907, played at Dungarvan on the 21st June, 1908, between
Cork and Kilkenny. Writing to the **Cork ‘Examiner’’ in.
May 1921, our revered friend, in a lengthy article, says:—
“T had not visited Ireland for fourteen years before that
day, and the swiftness and science of I);zlnnd’s national
game enthralled and astonished me. And there was Do
roughness or foul play. I saw some great hurling matches.
after that one, but T like to tell myself that these two
teams wero the greatest hurlers that Treland ever produced,
and that this mateh for All-Treland honours fought at Dun-
garvan on June 21st, 1908, was the greatest and the fastest
hurling 1nateh ever playéd on Treland’s ground.

| do not think that either of these two teams—the

Gork or the Kilkenny men—were superior to the other.
They were so even in gkill and strength that time alone
decided the game.

“ Tt was a battle of giants and clean and clever strilong
was the rule, - There was such gkill ¢hown that no man was
retired from injuries during the game and very few plajyers
were even seratched. There were alternate scores for either
team, and only by sheer Tuck the Kilkenny men lad their -
turn of scoring over when the whistle blew I would love
to see and speak to all the boys of that Cork and Kilkenny
crowd that had enthralled us that June day of 1908 by their
insurpassable exhibition of Trel
God bless them all wherever they ‘are.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.AA,

(CHAPTER XIV)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1922

No sincere Irishman can write of the year 1922 without
a feeling of regret and humiliation. The splendid national
organization that had been patiently and perseveringly built
up for the preceding five years was broken into fragments.
Men who had fought and suffered side by side found them-
selves now in opposite camps, displaying a bitter mutual
batred that could scarcely be conceived a fow months before.
Worse still, the phantom of Civil War loomed up on the
Trish horizon and, a few months later, stalked the land
leaving death and destruction in its trail. Irish blood was
spilled by brother-Trishmen and the sacred name of
Nationality was defiled by dark and terrible deeds.

One organization alone remained that claimed the
loyalty of both sides and that proved a leaven in the con-
fused and conflicting mass. That was the Gaelic Athletic
Association. Many prominent Gaels went down in this
foolish fratricidal strife, and we mention the names of
Michael Colling and Harry Boland as typical of the pro-
minent G.A.A, men who were lost to Ireland as a result of
this disastrous conflict. With commendable determination
the higher officials of the G.A.A. decided on carrying on,
and we must pay just tribute to the Munster and Leinster
Councils, who took up the broken links of the championships
and in spite of difficulties, made a manly effort to keep the
-games going.

The 1920 finals were not played until this year, when
Dublin defeated a Cork selection in hurling in May, and
‘Tipperary defeated the O'Tooles of Dublin in the football
final in June.

Everything considered, the G.A.A. has every reason to
be proud of its eforts in 1622, for it alone provided a com-
mon arena for fellow-Irishmen and did much to close the

ugly gap caused by the civil war.
‘The Treaty Ratified.

By 64 votes to 57 the Anglo-rish Treaty was ratified
on Saturday, 7th January, 1922, There were tense scenes

_in the Dail as the concluding stages were reached and when

the members began to record their votes for or against.
201

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.

Mr. Grifiith said 98 per cent. of the people were for ratifica-
tion, and as far asp?xe was concerned the Southern and:
Ulster Unionists would have fair play. Mr. de Valera:
broke down in ‘endeavouring to address the Dail and was
unable to comtinue. Mr. Michael Collins expressed the
general view, that the passing of the motion was not
regarded as a triumph over the other side. Mr. Collins also
appealed for unity and paid an impressive tribute to Mr.
de Valera.

Miss McSweeney, in an emphatic protest, declared that
this was the grossest act of betrayal Ireland ever endured,
and said she would never in any way help the Free State.

O’Brien, on his release from prison. It was dec!fled to-
continue the 1920 ehampionships, when Cork aml ZLimerick

Tramore Shrine Unveiled.

A beautiful shrine to the memory of two soldiers of the
Irish Republic—Michael McGrath and Tom O'Brien—was
unveiled at Tramore on Sunday, 5th March, by Mr. Cathal
Brugha, before a huge assembly. In the course of his
spirited address, Cathal Brugha made a strong appeal for
the language and quoted De Wett at the end of the Boer
‘War when he said:—'‘As long as we have our Janguage
there is no fear for our country! As long as we have our
language, no matter what may happen, we are not finally
beaten or subdued ; but if the {rish language dies, then the
Trish nation is dead.

““ Remember, you people who see that shrine: remem-
ber that they to whose memory it is erected died for a
certain ideal. Up to a few months ago we were all of one
mind with regard to the ideal that these men died for, and
they died for nothing else.”” (Applause.)

_ The inscription on the shrine is as follows: —* Erected
in memory of the Republican soldiers killed in action,

;;Imi,m}’r Tth, 1921: Michael McGrath and Tom O'Brien.

lodged by Limerick to Cork in connection with the Munster
Final of 1920. Eventually a settlement was made to the
satisfaction of all present. ¥t was fo the effect that. the

objéction and counter-objection be witidrawn. ~After a brief
discussion the delegates from the other counties acquiesced

Famous Fenian Dead. :
The death is now recorded (June) of Colonel Ricard
0’Sullivan-Burke, a native of Dunmanway, Co. Cork. He
served in the American Civil War and later became pro-
minent in Fenian circles. He it was, who planned the raid
i on Chester Castle, and later took part in the rescue of Kelly
i R and Deasy in Manchester. He was an engineer by pro-
! fession, and remained an extremist to the end. R.IP.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

Qutbreak of Civil War.
On Thursday morning, 29th June, the fvet shet the
disastrous Trish Civil War was fired when the Free State
Army invested the Your Courts, Dublin, and called on the
oceupants to surrender. An English 18-pounder gun, lent
for the oceasion by General Macready, shelled the building,
and a conflagration was started that enveloped all Ireland
a few weeks later.
Death of Cathal Brugha.
One of the most unbending and uneompromising Gaels
of our time passed away in the Mater Hospital, Dublin, on
Friday morning, 7th July, in the person of Cathal Brugha,
T.D. for Waterford. His death was_the result of wounds
received in action in Dublin against Free State forces. He
had been long identified with the Trish national movement,
speaker as well as a stern fighter

and was a splendid Irish
for Trish freedom. Beannacht De lena Anam!

in England and was one of the deputies to vote against the
Treaty. R.IP. .

ing. The All-Ireland football <emi-tinal between Tipperary
or Kerry and Mayo was fixed for Croke Park on Sunday,
8th April. Dublin having defeated Monaghan (Ulster), will
meet the winners in the final. The All-Ireland Hurling
Final (1921) between Dublin and Limerick has been fixed
for Croke Park on Sunday, 4th March. A statement of
accounts was submitted showing that the financial condition
of the Council was in a fairly satisfactory condition. A
bonus of £5 to senior teams and £3 to junior teams for each
match played was granted. A subscription of £100 was
passed 1o the O'Nowlan testimonial and a grant of £10 to
the All-Treland Schools’ Athletic Championships for the
past season. Permission was granted to the Kevin Barry
Memorial Committee for & hurling mateh between Kilkenny
and Dublin on Sunday, 4th February.

Athletic Club for Waterford.

A meeting was held in the City Hall, Waterford, on
Yriday evening, February 2nd, to form a branch of the
LN.A.CA. Mr. Willie Walsh, G.A.A., presided, and also
present were Peter O’Connor, Matt Flannelly, R. Kirwin,
M. Gallagher, T. Nush, A. J. Whitney, T. King, J. Murphy,
as well as a large number of local sportsmen.

Kilkenny Hurler Married.

The marriage is now published (February) of Mr. Pat
Aylward (“Dexter”), Knockmoylan, to Miss Bridie Walsh
of Castlegannon, Ballyhale. Mr, Aylward was a prominent
member of the Kilkenny All-Treland team of 1922, and
later played with Dublin and with the American team in
the Tailteann Games. He died last year (1930) in America
after a long and tedious illness. R.I.P.

Items of the Times.

In the third round of the 1922 Kilkenny junior hurling
championship Callan No. 1 defeated Knockmoylan—1-3 to
nil—at Knocktopher on Sunday, 18th February.

Mr. T. Walsh presided at a meeting of the Kilkenny
County Board on Sunday, 25th February. The following
delegates were appointed to represent Kilkenny at the
Leinster Convention:—Messrs. P, Lan‘gan, T. Walsh, J.
Roberts, and E. Purcell. Delegates to Congress : —Messrs.
T. Walsh, M. Phelan, P. Walsh, M, McDonald.

Tn the Kilkenny Northern Divisional hurling final,
played at St. James's Park on Sunday, 25th February,
Clomanto defeated Tullaroan after a fast and vigorous dis-
play—2-2 to 1 goal.

Re-organizing South Tipperary.

A meeting for the above object was held at the Hall,
Clonmel, on Sunday, 5th August, with Mr. Jerry Shelly in
the chair. Delegates attended from the following clubs : —
Killenaule, Clonmel, Cahir, Fethard, Powerstown, Bally-
fowloo, Grangemockler, Ballyneale, Mullinahone, Killustry,
and Carrick. " It was decided to circularise all the clubs in
the division to a meeting on August 15th at Clonmel, when
affiliations would be received and draws take place.

Munster Final 1922,

The replay of the Munster Hurling Final between Tip-
perary and Limerick took place on Sunday, 12th August,
at Limerick before a crowd of 20,000. The previous game
at Thurles on July 1st resulted in a draw of 2-2 each. Sun-
day’s gate receipts totalled £1,363, and ‘‘the contest will
rank as the most Homeric encounter recorded in thé annals
of the game. Its pace was terrific and the casualty list
abnormal.”  Final scores:—’l‘ipper‘sry, 4-2; Limerick, 1-4,

All-Ireland Hurling Final 1922,

Galway put up a splendid fight against Tipper: in
the hurling semi-final of the lmgchamgpionship l:lt‘ G’:Kvay
on Sunday, 26th August, before a crowd of 4,000 people.
Heavy rain militated much against the display. At halt
time the scores were: —Tipperary, 3-2; Galway, 1-1. In
the second half Tipperary failed to score, whilst Galway put
up two points, leaving ‘the final scores :—Tipperary, 8-2;
Galway, 1-8. For Galway, King, Morris, Keogh, and Har-
vey were outstanding. .

Munster 1923 Semi-finals.

n senior and junior football played at Cork on Sunday,
2nd September, Kerry defeated Cork in senior fcotball—
3.4 to 3 points; and Cork had an easy victory in the junior
event—3-6 to 1 point. The gate receipts were £80.

‘Munster Council.

A meeting of the Munster Council was held in Cork on
Sunday, 2nd September, Mr. J. O'Brien in the chair. An
application from the Gaelic League for matches m aid was
_acceded to, and the following teams were selected to play
inter-county games for this deserving appeal : —Tipperary,
Kerry, Limerick, and Cork.

Championship Fixtures.
The following championship fixtures for 1923 were
made :—Clarp v. Tipperary: S.H. and J.F., 23rd Septem-
ber at Clonmel; Tipperary v. Limerick (hurling)—Crowe
Memorial, 30th September ; Cork v. Limerick: 8. and J.H.,
at Thurles, 7th October; Cork v. Tipperary: J.F., 14th
-QOctober; Kerry v. Tipperary: S.F., 14th October.

A Famous Hurling Final (1922).
One of the greatest and most exciting tussles ever waged
“for the premier hurling title took place at Croke Park on
-Sunday, 9th September, when Kilkenny had a sensational
Jast-minute victory over Tipperary in the final for the 1922
.championship. A huge crowd attended and twenty-seven
-special trains ran to the capital. ‘‘Alternate scoring, clean,
hard hitting, with clashing, splintering ash, wielded by the
greatest experts in the land; electric movement from goal
to goal ; frequent blade to blade duels for possession ; quickly
changing fortunes—yet, for all, a struggle fought out to the
-end with splendid, though relentless spirit. These were the
features of a game which will be ranked as perhaps the best
-played in the hurling code of modern times.”’

All-Ireland Football Final 1922. .
Tn the All-Tréland Football Semi-final played at Croke
Park on September 9th, Sligo defeated Tipperary, but were
cubsequently defeated by Galway in the Connacht final;
hence the meeting of Dublin and Galway in the All-Ireland
Final. ‘‘The game was well-contested all through, but
Dublin finished the stronger of the two teams.”” There
were twenty special trains to Dublin for the game; 12,000
people attended and the gate receipts were £988. The
final scores were—Dublin, 6 points; Galway, 4 points. The
teams were ! —

Munster Football Finals.

Played at Tralee on Sunday, 14th October, before a
large crowd, Tipperary defeated Cork in the junior football
final—2-5 to 1-3; and after a splendid, close and robust
game Kerry won the Munster Football Championship by de-
feating Tipperary—6 points to 3 points.

In the senior event Tipperary led by 2 points to nil ab
the interval, but typical dash brought the Kerry men to
vietory on the above scores.

Mr. W. P. Ahearn, Cork, refereed the senior event, and
M. D. Bailey, Kerry, the junior contest.

Kilkenny County Final—a Draw.

After a splendid game played at St. James's Park,
Kilkenny, on Sunday, 21st October, Clonmore (Mconcoin)
played a drawn game with Clonmanto for the final .of the
Kilkenny Senior Championship. The final scores were—
6.2 each. On both sides were many of the players who
participated in the All-Treland Final against Tipperary in
September.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

(CHAPTER XVI)
THE ASSOCIATION IN 1924

The year 1924 was rather a difficult one for the G.A.A,
in general. Owing to the civil war and its disastrous con-
sequences, the championships lapsed and a big effort was
made to bring matters up to normal again. Though at one
period it looked as if the 1923 finals were to be abandoned,
a special Congress in August decided otherwise, with the
result that the sequel was a happy and satisfactory one.
Three All-Ireland Finals were played this year—two in
hurling and one in football. After years of gallant effort,
Galway won their first All-Treland (1928) at the expense of
Limerick, but later that year succumbed to Dublin for the
. 1924 title. Dublin defeated Kerry for the football title after
one of the finest exhibitions of the native code ever played
in ‘an Irish arena. It was Kerry's first appearance in the
final since they were vanquished by Wexford in 1915,

One of the most important motions ever passed by the
ruling bodg' was put into force this year and had a splendid
effect on the general conduct of the games. I refer to the
rule regarding punciuality. For years the non-obscrvance
of this important item had a very serious effect on the games
and particularly with regard to public patronage. Many
- who otherwise would have supported native pastimes re-
 frained from attending due to the fact that they were usually
kept an hour waiting before the proceedings started, and
oftentimes under adverse weather conditions. Happily, un-
punctuality is now a thing of the past and Gaelic games are
started to-day in strictly scheduled time, with a consequent
increase in public esteem and patronage.

For the two important reasons I have mentioned the
ear 1924 may be written down as a highly successful one
from a Gaelic standpoint and may be said to be the turning
point in present-day G.A.A. history. From 1918 to 1923
there were many causes that proved detrimental to the
roper conduct of native pastimes, and it is a pleasure to
record that the Gaelic barque was righted again in 1924 and
nder full sail steered its natural and true national course.

All-Ireland Congress—The Ban.

At the Annual Congress held in Dublin on Easter
Sunday, 20th April, the principal motion on the agenda was
to rescind the ban on foreign games, which was defeated by
54 votes to 82. Rev. Fr. Fogarty, 0.P., Newbridge, and'
Mr. Barney Fay of Cavan spoke in favour of its retention,
whilst Mr. Dan McCarthy, Chairman, said it was their duty
to create a good feeling amongst all Irishmen. G.A.A.
players, he stated, had not a monopoly of patriotism, and
he believed if a plebiscite amongst the players were taken
the rule would be rescinded. Mr. P. D. Breen (Wexford)
said in his opinion the inclusion of the two rules mention
was an insult to the Gaels of Treland. Mr. Mulholland'
(Cork) said Congress would be doing a good work to delete
these rules. Mr. Drumgoole (Dublin) also spoke in favour
of the abolition of the ban. The motion was defeated by
B4 votes to 82, and the rules on the Official G.A.A. Rule
Book allowed to stand.

The motion to include the National Army as a Province
of the G.A.A. was defeated.

The scope of the Secretary’s yeport included Croke
Park, the Central Council, the Dr. C.oke Memorial, the

gseated stands could accommodate 1,080 people, and the

Death of Alderman Nowlan,

The death oceurred on Monday, 80th June, of Alderman
Seumag O'Nowlan, for twenty years president of the G.A.A.
A native of Kilkenny, he was during his lifetime a prominent
member of the extreme Nationalist Party, and was interned
-after Easter Week. He had a life-long connection with the
G.A.A.; was president of the Leinster Council from 1900 to
1905, and on the death of Michael Deering (of Cork) in 1901,

‘he succeeded him as chairman of the Central Council until
1921. The O'Nowlan Park in Kilkenny was subscribed to
-as & memorial to this great Gael —R.I.P,

Important Motions from Central Council,

The following motions were passed at a meeting of the
Central Council held on 17th August, 1924, and sent to all
Councils and County Committees, to be enforced from the
Ist September: (1)’ Teams failing to take the field at the
appointed hour to forfeit the match, even though winners
on the score; (2) Vigilance Committees to be appointed to
report on attendance of G.A.A. members at foreign games;
(8) Recommendation to all Councils and Committees to pro-
vide counter attractions when and where foreign games ara
played; (4) To set up Referees’ Associations ; (5) To devise
schemes with the heads of schools and colleges to bring the
students more under the control of the Association.

Munster Council.

A meeting of the Munster Council was held on Cork on
Sunday, 7th September, Mr. J. O’Brien presiding. It was
decided to give £20 to the Tim Nagle testimonial. On a poll,
Dungarvan was selected as the venue for the hurling finals,
date, the 5th October.

Galway Hurling Chamipons (1923).

After forty years of praiseworthy effort, the gallant men
of the West, from Galway (Connaught) brought back the
All-Treland blue riband of hurling for 1923 by defeating
Limerick, the 1921 champions, at Croke Park, on Sunday,
14th September. The crowd was not so large as in the pre-
vious year, when Kilkenny won a sensational game
from Tipperary. Galway played with great speed and dash
and and their robust tactics completely upset the Limerick
men,

TFinal scoros :—Galway, 7-3; Limerick, 4-5.

In the senior football semi-final played on same date
and tveuue, Tullogher defeated Cotterstown—3 goals to 1
point.

All-Ireland Football Final, 1923.

One of the greatest football finals ever played took place
at Croke Park on Sunday, 28th September, when Dublin
defeated Kerry by two points. It was Kerry’s first npgen-
ance in a final since 1915, when they were defeated by Wex-
ford. Twenty-nine special trams brought huge crowds to
the capital, and long bafore the appointed time, the
grounds were' packed. At the end of the first half a goal
by Brosnan gave Kerry the lead. ~‘There were thrills galore
and the rival supporters did not spare themselves in urging
.on their respective sides. The Kerry men finished with their
characteristic dash, and were only beaten when the final
whistle sounded. The game will rank as one of the classic
struggles for All-TIreland football honours.”

inal scores :—Dublin, 1-3; Kerry, 1-3.

‘Munster Finals, 1924, ‘
After a close, dour, and determined struggle Tipperary
won the Munster Hurling Championship from Limerick
‘All-Treland runners-up) at Dungarvan on Sunday, 5th
otober, by a margin of two points. The match from
start to stop was stubbornly contesed and both teams
fought to the finish. Both teams turned over with four
ints. In the second period Limerick led by a point, and
a goal to Tipperary brought them vietory.

(CHAPTER XVII)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1925

The year 1925 was not an oufstanding one in G.A.A.
The 1924 All-Treland Football Final was played in
April of this year, when Kerry defeated Dublin, the previous
three years' winners, by one point. In hurling Tipperary
gained the premier title by defeating Galway, who defeated
Kilkenny in the semi-final. This was Tipperary's tenth
‘All-Treland title, which to-date constituted a record, Cork
bhaving eight and Kilkenny a similar number.

The Football Championship for the year was not
finished owing to objections and counter-objections. The
four teams involved were Kerry, Cavan, Mayo, and Wex-
ford. The latter’s objection to Mayo was defeated, and
Mayo were temporary champions until their defeat by
Galway in the Connacht final, after which the latter were
officially declared All-Treland Football Champions for 1925,

The standard of the games was well maintained and
public patronage was generously bestowed. This year, too,
the National Hurling and Football Leagues were established
and provided a series of competitions that enabled the coun-
ties to keep in training and, incidentally, help them to dis-
cover new talent.

The rule of giving a player option to play for his native
county was also put into force this year. At the Annual
Congress the motion to lift the ban was defeaed by 69 votes

to 23.

Father Albert’s Last Message,

In his last letter to Ireland, addressed to Mr. Bamonn
de Valera, Rev. Father Albert, the patriotic and militant
Franciscan, wrote : —

_“ From my deathbed T salute you and wish you to know that
I die a citizen of the Irish Rc‘public, unchanged and unchangeable,
and uncompromising in my allegiance to that Republic and to you,
#s President.”

Coneluding, he writes:—

. Would that I had died in the land of my love, but God’s
will be done. I have loved and desired justice for my native land
and hated the cruel imﬂnty of England’s oppression, and I feq
that I can say with St. Gregory: ‘I have loved justice and hated
iniquity ; therefore, I die in exile.”

Cork (who defeated Tipperary in Cork) and Limerick
who defeated Clare at Limerick) met at Waterford in the

ing scores:—Cork, 2-8; Limerick, 2 points.

sum was handed over to the Ulster Council.

Archbishop Mannix in Kilkenny.

Archbishop Mannix arrived in Kilkenny on Monday
evening, August 17th, to receive the Freedom of the City,
and was accorded a tremendous ovation. On the route
from 'Mellemg' to the City he was greeted on all sides by
spontaneous demonstrations of the people, who appreciated
his efforts as a champion of their rights and who were not
oblivious of his efforts in the cause of self-determination for
Treland during the Black and Tan regime.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

{CHAPTER XVIIT)

THE ASSOCIATION IN 1926

The year 1926 was a vintage one in G.A.A. annals,
The sun of success beamed benignly from every quarter.
The games were good ; rivalry was keen; rigid punctuality
-was observed ; public patronage was liberal and gate returng
-most flattering. The year opened well with a classic dis.
play of hurling at Cork in May between the locals and
Dublin in the first final of the National Hurling League. In
the first round of the Leinster Championship, at New Ross,
another thrilling and beautiful display of the national pas-
time was given when Kilkenny defeated Dublin by "the
minimum margin. It took three games to finish the Mun-
ster final between Cork and Tipperary. The displays given
were not as brilliant as the above-mentioned. Rather they
-were dour,dogged displays that have always been the out-
come when “Rebel Cork” and *Gallant Tipperary”” test
‘timber and temper for the Munster hurling title,

The Central Council was incidentally lucky by having
two games for the football final between the old rivals,
Kerry and Kildare, the first having resulted in a draw.
The dual meeting brought in a return of roughly £6,000.
The Munster Council, too,, had an unlooked for share of
Auck in the three games for the Munster hurling crown,

The tour of the Tipperary hurlers was another out-
«standing event in G.A.A, nnns,{s. The tour was a splendid
-success and happy results were achieved. The splendid dis-
plays of the Tipperary men thrilled thousands of our exiled
~countrymen and also brought splendi

time rivals, Kilkenny, before a concourse of 26,000 at Croke
] alrk; and in football Kerry won the championship from
ildare.
Altogether the year 1926 was an outstanding one in
-A.A. annals, and ‘the historian of the future can mark
down this year as the beginning of a great cycle in the
istory of our National Athletic Association.

Annual Congress.

The Annual Congress was held at Croke Park on Easter
Sunday, 8rd April, when the proceedings lasted the entire
day. Mr. P. D. Breen, Wexford, presided. The state-
ment of accounts and auditors’ reports were unanimously
adopted, as well as the reports from the four Provincial
Councils. Mr. W. P. Clifford (Limerick) was elected Pre-
sident by 68 votes to 55 for Mr. Breen.

On the proposition of the new Chairman, the term of

office was limited to two years.
The motion by the Dublin Board to remove the han

was beaten by a big majority.

The Inter-Provincial matches were decided upea, and
it was also decided that if at the termination of a repiny the
scores were level, fifteen minutes each way must be played.
A special committee of the Central Council was appointed

to all irregularities and report same to Council.

Cahill, President of the Chicago G.A-A., the Tipperary
team (All-Treland champions) toured the States this year-
and made international hurling history. Their trip was &
geries of successes from New York to the Golden Gate, and
everywhere the Tipperary men were received with over-
whelming enthusiasm. This was the first occasion sincer
1888 that an Irish team toured the States. Seven matches-
in all were played and in every case the visitors emerged
victors. On their return to their native land the hurlers-
were welcomed as conquering heroes, and their native Tip-

The death is recorded in Dublin (J: uly) of ex-Alderman
J. McCarthy of Kilkenny, who was at one period closely
identified with the G-A.A. and was an official handicapper
and referee. R.IP.

In the second round of the Leinster Junior Hurling
-Championship, Leix defeated Kilkenny at St. James's Park,
Kilkenny, on Sunday, 17th July—4-2 to 1-4.

On Sunday evening, 24th July, the Tipperary nurlers
arrived back at Cobh after their successful tour of the
States. They travelled from New York to San Francisco
and played seven matches in all, winning every one. They
received a generous welcome in every centre of the States
and were feted and feasted like conquering heroes. A sum-
mary of their tour appears elsewhere in this chapter.

In the semi-final of the Kilkenny Senior Championship
Mooncoin defeated Kilmacow at Waterford on Sunday, 24th

-July, by 8-4 to 1-1. Mr. Peter Walsh was referee and the
gate receipts were £100,

In the Waterford County semi-final, at Dungarvan,
Dungarvan beat Ferrybank in’senior hurling by 8-4 to 2.1,

;nd in junior grade Lismore defeated Ballytruckle by 4-2 to
-2.

For the Kilkenny senior hurling title, played on Sun-
day, 7th August, at Kilkenny, Dicksboro’ defeated Mooncoin.
by 5-5 to 1-4 after a fine exhibition and before a great crowd.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

Munster Semi-final- &

Before an enormous crowd, estimated at 15,000, Tip-
perary defeated Limerick in a hard gruelling game on Sun-
day, 21st August, for the semi-final of the Munster Senior
Hurling Championship. The game was stubbornly contested
from start to stop and Tipperary were lucky to secure two-
goals towards the finish, which brought them victory. Final
scores : —Tipperary, 6-5; Limerick, 4-6. Mr. Paddy O'Keefle
was referee, and the gate receipts were over £600. Ine
junior football on the same day Tipperary defeated Lime-
rick on the scores 1-8 to 1-2.

All-Ireland Semi-final—Kilkenny Defeat Galway.

After a fast and robust game at Croke Park, on Sunday,
29th August, Kilkenny qualified for the All-Treland Hurling
Final by defeating Galway by 6-2 to 5-1 before a crowd of
10,000. Kilkenny led at one period by 8-1, but the forceful
play of the Connacht men almost wiped out the deficit.
Diak Grace played a great game for Kilkenny, and also-
John Roberts, who scored five goals.

All-Ireland Football Final—A Draw.

Kerry and Kildare played a drawn game at Croke Park
on Sunday, 5th September, for the final of the All-Treland
Football Championship, before a huge attendance. Final’
scores :—Kerry, 1-3; Kildare, 0-8. (For teams see replay.)’

“Drug"” Walsh Beneiit,

For the benefit of the above veteran hurler two matches
were played at Waterford on Sunday, 5th September, when
Mooncoin defeated James Stephens 2-4 to 22 (Mr. Peter
Walsh referee), and Tullaroan defeated Dicksboro’ 7-1 to
2.4 (Mr. Sean Hogan referee). The gate receipts were:
£114.

Munster Final Fiasco.

Owing to the encroaching of the crowd, which assumed
enormous proportions, the Munster hurling final between.
Corls and Tipperary had to be declared off on Sunday, 12th
September. After twenty minutes’ play Tipperary were:
leading by 1-2 to nil. In the junior football final Kerry
defeatod Tipperary by 1-5 to 2-1.

Munster Final.

Cork and Tipperary played a drawn game at 13 points.
each at Thurles on the 19th September before a record’
crowd. Cork, 3-4; Tipperary, 4-1,

“Items of This Period.

In the Kilkenny County Junior Hurling Final played
-on Sunday, 13th February, Knockmoylan defeated Conahy
by 8-5 to 8 goals. ‘““From the outset it was a thrilling
struggle—dash, skill, speed, and grit being manifest at
.every turn.””

Kilkenny Senior Draws:—Kilmacow v. Mooncoin;
Danesfort v. James Stephens; Tullaroan v. Dicksboro’;
Knockmoylan, a bye.

At a meeting of the Kilkenny County Board, a com-
mittee consisting of the Chairman (Mr. T. Walsh) and
Messrs. Holohan, Heffernan, Davin, Joyce, Roberts, Butler,
-and Gibbons were appointed to enquire into the feasibility
«of purchasing a new Gaelic ground for the city.

A very enjoyable function was held in the Militar
‘Barracks, Kilkenny, Commandant James Hanrahan presid-
~ ing, on Saturday night, 12th February, when four silver

«cups won by the Army teams were presented. Two of the
+cups were won by the hurlers of the Eastern Corzmand, and
~ the other two were for efficiency and for the best company
in the 5th Brigade. There were several visitors from the
-city and a most enjoyable night was spent.

 Kilkenny Defeat Cork at Waterford.
In a game for the benefit of the Ulster Council and for
“a set of gold medals, Kilkenny defeated Cork (All-Ireland
~Champions) after a splendid game played at Waterford on
 Sunday, 20th February. The scores were equal at half
time with one goal each, but Kilkenny played true to tradi-
“tion in the second half and ran out deserving winners on the
-scores—Kilkenny, 5 goals; Cork, 1-1. Mr. Scan Hogan,

TEARS OF 1 = —
The First Branch of the G.A.A. in Kilkenny.

A meeting to start a branch of the G.A.A. in Kilkenny:
was held at the grocery establishment of Mr. P. Geary,
Rose Inn Street, on Wednesday evening, Ttn July, 1885.
Mr. James Wade, T.C., occupied the chair, and others pre-
sent were :—Messre, Whittaker, Ed. Callanan, P. Rowan,
S. Donleavy, P. J. O'Keeffe, M. MecGrath, W. Sherwood,
John Cleers. More than one hundred applications for mem-
bership were received.

Munster Convention.
The annual Munster Convention was held in Thurles on.
Sunday, 13th March, with Mr, Jerry O’Brien (Clare) in the
chair. -In a vigorous speech, Mr. Clifford, of Limerick,
President of the Central Council, condemned the statements
of some nrdent Rugbyites who lately expressed the fear that
the game was getting too popular and that Lansdowne Road
would become another Jones’s Road. Mr. Clifford reminded.
the Rugby advocates that TLansdowne Road was never
visited by the Auxiliaries, and he objected strongly to any-
body belonging to the old ascendancy or ex-ascendancy re-
ferring to what they called the ‘*conduct’’ at Jones's Road.
or Croke Park.
Total income for the year; £6,445 11s. 8d., and expen-
diture £5,609 2s. 3d., leaving an excess of income over
expenditure of £746 9s. 5d. The assets in the balance-sheet
on the 10th February, 1927, consisted of cash in bank on
deposit account £1,000, and loans outstanding £475, making
a total of £1,475. The liabilities on the same date amounted!
to £122 15s. 9d. The balance to credit of Council at the
end of the year was £1,352 4s. 3d.
Messrs, Frank McGrath, Sean Og Murphy, and Dick.
Fitzgerald were elected to the Central Council.
The draws were as follows : —Cork v. Kerry;
v. Tipperary ; ‘Waterford v. Clare.

interesting one, and Tipperary won in traditional style.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

It was unanimously adopted that busts of the late
Maurice Davin, Michael Cusack, and Most Rey, Dr. Croke
‘be erected over the main entrance of Croke Park,

By a big majority Congress adopted the decision of the
‘Central Council and called on the Connacht Council to hand
over the medals for the 1925 Football Championship to
Galway.

Satisfactory reports were received from the four Pro-
vincial Councils and same adopted by Congress. Mr. J.
‘0'Brien, Chairman of the Munster Council, stated that 1926
was a record year in Munster.

"TAILTEANN GAMES,

There was a long discussion regarding representation
-on the Tailteann Council, when Mr, Sean McCarthy, Cork,
stated that no elenients in this country should be allowed to
predominate over the Irish-Treland movements. Mr. D, P,
Walsh (Tipp.) said they wanted nothing at Croke Park buf
‘what was Irish-Treland. General O'Duffy said the G.A.A.
-should not be satisfied with one or two representatives on
the Tailteann Council; they should have fifty. Mr. Pat
MecGrath stated that the Munster Council would erect a
monument over the grave of Maurice Davin, first President
‘of the G.A.A.

FINANCE.

Receipts for the year, £13 894 17s. 10d. (a record), as
compared with £6,505 1s. 8d. for 1925 and £6,246 8s. 10d.
for 1924. Earnings in the form of rents amounted to £2,798
2s. 9d;, s compared with £1,434 18s. 5d. for 1925 and
£2,426 19s. 4d. for 1924. Forty-five new turnstiles, at a
cost of £900, were installed during the year, as well as
‘seating accommodation for over 7 000 people.

Clubs affiliated, 1,317, compared with 1,196 in 1925
and 1,051 in 1924

After dischargiag their linbilities in connection with the
various competitions under their control the Central Council
was in & position to transfer to the Croke Park account the
-sum of £3,000, thus reducing the bank overdraft to £3,023
12s. 9d., and Leinster Council £1;150.

Liabilities, £4,614 3s.; teams’ expenses for the year,
including grants, £2,927 7s. 6d., as follows: —Kerry, £40;
Kildare, £800; Kilkenny, £300; Cork, £100; Connacht
“Council, £100; Armagh, £25. Grants of £200 were made
to the Drumcollogher Relief Fund and the Handball Central
Council.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

“The March of the Gaels.”

Commenting on the Congress, the KILKENNY JOURNAL
states:—'* Last Sunday's Congress of the G.A.A. was-
heartening and uplifting. It breathed of self-reliant nation-
ality and confidence in the future of the true Gaelic State.
All Treland was represented and that cosmos which is Ireland.
found expression, when needed, at every phase of the pro-
ceedings. Youth was predominant, but it was youth which
had not detached itself from old colleagues or old ideals. Tt
was Gaeldom in a quiet, transacting mood—conscious of its
duties and not unmindful of its chivalry.

“The G.A.A. is strong. Adfter forty years it survives
vitalised by the blood of each mew generation, It has not’
Jorgotten and cannot forsake its purpose while it exists, and
is animated by the spirit that Easter Sunday evoked. TIre-
land cannot die and will not surrender to bondage.”

- The Whitsuntide Tour.

This year saw the inauguration of the annual tour of

 two leading Irish hurling teams to Liverpool and London.

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

The old rivals, Cork and Tipperary, filled the bill and two
splendid games were played in the above centres. A crowd
roughly estimated at 10,000 attended at both places and the
games were played in the spirit of championship fixtures.
he final scores at Liverpool were:—Cork, 8-3; Tipperary,
5-8. At London—Cork, 4-5; Tipperary, 3 points.

nature of a born hurler to play a friendly game. He was
like a greyhound after an electric hare. When he saw &
ball he must 2o after it.”’ (Laughter.)

At the conclusion of the game in TLondon a cup was
handed to the victors by Mr. Liam MecCarthy of the London
G.A.A., aud both teams received a set of medals. Messrs.
P. D. Breen, Central Couneil; Paddy O’Keeffe, and
“Widger” Meagher accompanied the teams. A gold medal
was presented to Mr. Liam MecCarthy by Mr. P. 0'Keeffe
as a souvenir of the hurlers’ visit to Tondon.

0'Nowlan Park Purchased.

The Gaels of Kilkenny have now purchased (June) &
splendid field from Mr. Peter Corcoran for £700, to be
known as the O’Nowlan Park in memory of Alderman
James Nowlan, a Kilkenny Gael and President of the G.A.A.
for twenty years. Towards the equipment of the field it
was decided to make a levy of £1 on each affiliated club.

Munster Hurling Semi-final,

Before a crowd estimated at 20,000 Cork beat Limerick
in the semi-final of the Munster Hurling Championship at
Thurles on Sunday, 24th July. Sixteen special trains ran
in connection with the match and the scene as the teams
took the field was an inspiring one. At half time the scores
- were equal at 2-2 each, but in the second period Cork took
command and ran out deserving winners on the scores:—

The G.A.A. and Irish.

In the weekly Irish-Treland Notes of this period refer-
ence is made to the objects of the G.A.A. in connection with
the language. ‘‘ Its founders never intended that it (the
G.A.A.) should be satisfied to confine its activities to the pre-
servation and fostering of the national pastimes. The move-
ment aime” at the support of the language, the assistance of'
Irish indus.mes—in a word, everything that could tend to
the re-establishment of the Gael in that proud position:
which he should hold in his own land. . . It is well to
assert that its ultimate object must be an organization
Gaelic in fact as well as in name.”

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

(CHAPTER XX)
THE ASSOCIATION IN 1928

The progress made and the popularity achieved by the
G.A.A. in the two preceding years was well maintained dur-
ing 1928. All-Treland honours were divided between Lein-
ster and Munster—Kildare winning the football title from
Cavan after one of the most exciting and closely contested
games ever played in the native code. In hurling, Corlc
agan brought back the honours to the South after defeating
Dublin at Kilkenny in the semi-final and later vanquishing
Galway in the final.

In Munster, Clare made a most praiseworthy effort for
the Munster senior hurling title, and their two games with
Cork, at Thurles, will be long remembered by the thousands
who were present. The hurling served up was of the highest:
quality and both games were fought to a finish in the best
traditions of Gaelic sportsmanship.

The Tailteann Games were held again this year in
Dublin and brought thousands of our exiled countrymen
from all parts of the globe. The hurling and football dis-
plays were thrilling contests and gave eloquent proof of the
vitality of Gaelic games amongst our kith and kin abroad.

The opening of the O’Nowlan Park in Kilkenny is
another item that must not be omitted in a history of this
kind. The opening day in August, when Cork and Dublin
clashed in the All-Treland hurling semi-final, saw a mighty
hosting of Gaeldom in the old Marble City, and the blessing
of the grounds by the Most Rev. Dr. Collier, Bishop of
Ossory, was a most impressive ceremony.

The Black North and Irish-Ireland.

There was a great Irish-Treland night in St. Mary's
Hall, Belfast, in the be%iun ng of the New Year, the fune-
tion being graced by the presence of his Lordship Most
Rev. Dr. McRory and his Grace Most Rev. Dr. Michaek
0’Doherty, Archbishop of Manilla. The Ceilidh was organ-
ised in furtherance of the movement for a closer alliance
between the G.A.A. and the Gaelic League.

Most Rev. Dr. McRory (now Patron of the G.A.A.)
expressed his delight at seeing such a huge gathering. *“You
are bere,”” he said, “doing a great work not only for your

Anmul Congress.

The Annual Congress of the Gaels was held in the
Central Hotel, Dublin, on Easter Sunday, 8th April, Mr. W,
Clifford in the chair, and delegates attended from thirty-one
counties. Mr. Sean Ryan (Dublin) was' elected President
by 92 votes to 33 for Mr. Sean T. Ruane (Mayo).

The six months’ suspension on Kerr

y _for refusing to
finish their mateh with Ulster in the provincial semi-final
was removed.

Messrs, P. D. Breen and T. F., Mulholland were re-
elected Trustees,

The reports and statements of accounts from the pro-

vinces showed that the year 1927 was a prosperous one for
the G.AA.

On the proposition of Mr. B. Fay (Cavan), Kildare
were admitted to the All-Treland Junior Football Final
against Cavan.

It was decided to send a deputation to the Minister for
Lands and Agriculture to urge the provision of playing-
grounds for schools with a view to popularising native games.

A Dublin motion that no player be allowed to participate
~ in a game unless properly “togged’” was carried.

Expenses for refreshments for teams in int
matches were increased from £5 10s, to £7 10s.

a credit balance of £27,365, made up of Central Council,
£15,061; and Croke Park, £12,304, Gate receipts from ten
venues in 1927 realised £2,364, whilst the football final
between Kerry and Kildare brought a ‘‘gate” of £8,364.

‘was £5764, the exces;

s of income over expenditure was

Munster Final—a Draw.

Before a huge attendance, estimated at 27,000, Clare
played a drawn game with Cork for the Munster final at
Thurles, on Sunday, 15th July. The game was one of the
finest ever played under G.A.A. auspices and a draw was &
fitting result. Cork, 2-2; Clare, 2-2.

By defeating Limerick on the same date and venue the
Waterford minors won the first inter-county minor contest
ever played under the auspices of the Munster Council.

Items of This Period,

A Teix football team that visited Waterford on Sunday,
22nd July, defeated a local selection by 1-6 to 2-2.

After the first games played in the O’Nowlan Memorial
Park, Kilkenny, on Sunday, 17th July, Mr. Peter Corcoran
(late owner of the grounds) ‘entertained the members of the
Kilkenny County Committee at his residence and a very
pleasant evening was spent.

‘At Gortnahoe Feis on Sunday, 15th July, Mullinahone
defeated The Commons in football by 2-1 to 1 goal, and
Tullaroan defeated Killenaule in hurling by 6-1 to 2-2.

A Verse for the Gaels.
Come forth, come forth, my gallant Gaels;
Be upright, fearless, steady;
Before calm strength rude discord pales,
Make ready, boys, make ready;
*Tis for the right you all unite,
Then let your watchwords be:
Courage and truth and stainless youth—
They'll make old Treland free!
{BrieN O’LEARY).
‘The Munster Final.

One of the greatest Munster finals ever played took
place between Cork and Clare at Thurles on Sunday, 29th
July, before a crowd of over 80,000, The day was beauti-
fully fine; crowds gathered from all parts of Ireland and
enthusiasm was at fever pitch, The game was strenuously
fought out, but Cork had taken advantage of their first
day's experience and put a perfect team on the field. At
half time Cork led by 2-4 to 1-2. The final scores were:—
Cork, 6-4; Clare, 2-2.

“Mhe contest was a great one and in every way worthy
of the best traditions of a Munster final.”

Mr. Jack Keoghan, one of the exile team, a native of
Tullaroan, played for Kilkenny against Cork in the All-
Treland final at Dungarvan in July, 1908.

American Gaels on Tour.

After the Tailteann Games, the American footballers
visited Tralee and played a drawn game with Kerry—1-7
to 2.4. It was a splendid exhibition of the native code,
fast, robust and fiery.

TLater they visited Templemore and defeated a Tip-
perary selection by 4-3 to 1-4 before a crowd of 3,000, on
Sunday, 2nd September.

The American hurlers visited Ennis on the same day
and defeated Clare by 4-7 to 2-6. The visitors received a
tremendous ovation. Mr, Mike Shortal, an old Kilkenny
All-Treland player, was in charge of the visiting hurlers.

Kilkenny Final.

After a series of objections the Kilkenny final in senior
hurling was played at Waterford for the third time on
Sunday, 19th August, between Mooncoin and James
Stephens. A heavy downpour stopped the game, and in the
ensuing trial Mooncoin won by 2-4 to 2-2 on Sunday, 23rd
September.

0'Nowlan Park Formally Opened.

Previous to the All-Treland hurling semi-final between
Cork and Dublin, at Kilkenny, on Sunday, August 26th, the
grounds were formally opened by Mr. Sean Ryan, President
of the G.A.A. The Corporation attended in state, headed
by Alderman John Magennis, Mayor. His Lordship the
Most Rev. Dr. Collier, Bishop of Ossory; a large number of
clergy, and representative Gaels from all over Ireland, also

were present. i

Mr, Ryan, in the course of his address, said they were
dedicating this Park to one of the finest Gaels they ever had m
Yhis couniry, and each and everyone of them knew that he was
the staunchest Gael that ever left Kilkenny. He played a big
part in the administration of the Association for a number of
Pears.  The late Alderman Nowlan was a straightforvard, honest
Tael. (May God rest his soul) This Park would be a living
montment to his work for the Association and to the strong
position in which it was to-day. 3

Bressive Tre Grouxps.—His Lordship the Most Rev. Dr.
Collier then came forward and blessed the grounds. After the

soremony his Lordship said they gave the blessing of = the
Church to the new Park, and in giving the blessing of the Church
he might remind the Gaels here gathored that they could hono

All-Ireland Hurling Semi-final.

Before a crowd estimated at 23,000, Cork (Munster
champions) had a big victory over Dublin (Leinster cham-
pions) at the O'Nowlan Park, Kilkenny, in the semi-final of
the All-Ireland Championship on Sunday, 26th August.

“‘The game was most disappointing from every point of
view and the display was not up to the standard one would
expect to see in an All-Treland semi-final. Dublin failed
hopelessly, and at no period did they hurl in winning vein.

The ball was thrown in by the Most Rev. Dr. Collier,
Bishop of Ossory, and amongst those present were Most
Rev. Dr, Keves, Bishop of Saramah, U.S.A., and the Earl'
of Ossory. Half time scores:—Cork, 2-2; Dublin, 1 point.
Cork’s more finished display and ready methods accounted
for their vietory. They completely outran and outplayed’
their opponents in every phase.”

Full time scores :—Cork, 5-3; Dublin, 2 points. Mr. Peter
Walsh, N.T., Kilkenny, refereed, and the teams were:—

The Kickham Centenary.

The centenary of the birth of Charles J. Kickham, poet,
novelist, and patriot, was celebrated by a huge demonstra-
tion at Mullinahone on Sunday, 2nd September. Contin-
gents attended from many counties and the proceedings
were marked by genuine enthusiasm. Many speeches were
delivered by public men and Mr. Frank Fahy gave the
oration at the graveside. Mr. Fahy paid a fitting tribute to
the gentle Fenian patriot, and quoted Kickham’s last words
in conclusion :—* I die in the Catholic faith and I die loving
Ireland ; I only wish I could have done more for her.””

All-Ireland Harling Final.

Played at Croke Park on Sunday, 9th September, be-
tween Cork and Galway, and before a crowd of 20,000
people, Cork were easily superior in all departments and
ran out winners on the scores :—Cork, 6-12; Galway, 1 goal.

““ Throughout the hour’s play the Galway men made
valiant efforts to hold their opponents, and at times in-
dividual players gave magnificent exhibitions and fielded
effectively. But there was no combined effort, and Cork
won in decisive style.”

G.A.A. Items.

Mr. Jim Walsh (‘“Builder”), captain of the Faughs H.C.,
and a well-known All-Treland hurler, has been elected Chair-
man of the Dublin County Board.

At Knocktopher on Sunday, 4th November, 1928, the
semi-final of the Kilkenny football championship was played
when Tullogher defeated Knockroe—1-4 to 1 goal.

At a meeting of the Central Council held on Saturday
night, 10th November, Ferrybank (Waterford) was cfficially
included in Kilkenny for G.A.A. purposes.

At a meeting of the Kilkenny County Board, the Chair-
man, Mr, Tom Walsh, recommended the use of Mr. Phil
O’Neill’s Trish phrase book for Gaelic games as & splendid
medium for popularising the language on the field of play.

In the League hurling tie at Port Laoighise, Leix de-
feated Wexford by 6-4 to 1-2.

For the Kilkenny Southern final, played on Sunday,
18th November, at Knocktopher, Tullogher defeated Glory
Rovers (Dunamaggin) by 8 points to 1 point.

On Sunday, 18th November, a memorial was unveiled
at Grangemockler to the memory of Michael Hogan, a
famous Tipperary footballer, who was shot by British forces
at Croke Park on ““Bloody Sunday’'—November 1920.

In the football league competition Carlow defeated
Wicklow by 62 to 1-4 at Bagenalstown on Sunday, 18th
November,

For the North Kilkenny junior hurling final Wellbrook
defeated Dickshoro’ by 5-7 to 6-3 on Sunday, 2nd December.

At the Kilkenny County Convention held on Sunday,
9th December, Mr. Sean Gibbons was unanimously elec
to the chair. Income for the year, £885 8s. 8d.; expendi-
ture, £504 12s. 10d., showing a balance of £380 15s. 5d. A
motion to remove the ban was defeated by 41 votes to 10.

In the semi-final of the South Kilkenny junior hurling
championship played at Mullinavat on Sunday, 16th Decem-
ber, Carrickshock defeated Kilmacow by 2-3 to 2-2.

Mr. Muiris Breathnac, Gaelic League organiser, Kil-
kenny, was drowned in the River Nore on Thursday, 27th
Jevember. Deceased was an earnest worker in the Trish-
1.eland movement. R.LP.

teated Leix by 5-1 to 4 goals on’ Sunday, 25th November.

beautiful gift, his Grace said that ‘‘the G.A.A. is weided to-

and Trish indusries. The games had fostered self-relianco-

in Irish youth, and taught them to be men ; they encournged i

Leinster Hurling Final.

Under most depressing conditions the Leinster senior
hurling final was played at New Ross on Sunday, 28th July,
between Kilkenny and Dublin. Tt was a great, fast game
packed with thrills and splendid passages of caman-craft.
It is regrettable to record, however, that the game was
declared null and void owing to the two teams having been
late coming on the field. Final scores:—Kilkenny, 8.6;
Dublin, 2.6. Mr. Bob O'Keeffe, Chairman of the Leinster
Council, was referee, and the teams were:—

-All-Ireland Football Final,

After a lapse of three years Kerry again annexed the
football title at the expense of their old rivals, Kildare, on
Sunday, 22nd September, at Croke Park. The match was
‘well up to the standard of both these famous teams, and
Kerry won a great game by the margin of three points.
Full time scores: —Kerry, 1-8; Kildare, 1-5.

There was a crowd of 43,000 present, and the gate
rreceipts—£4,010—constituted a record.

“ The vigour and determination of the Munstermen
iproved too much for the less forceful, if more polished,
-game of Kildare.”

Gaelic Night at Mooncoin.

A thoroughly Gaelic night took place in the Schoolhouse,
Mooncoin, on Wednesday, 11th September. The oceasion
was the distribution of the county championship medals won

“by Mooncoin for the year 1926 and 1927. Rev. Canon Doyle

presided, and amongst those present were :—Rev. Father
Dollard, D.Litt. (the poet-priest of the Gaels); Rev. Father
Matthew, O.S.F.C.; Messrs. Sean Gibbons, M.C.C.;
Seumas Hawe, Kilkenny, and Phil O’Neill.

A silver-mounted walking-stick, suitably inscribed, was
presented by the Gaels of Moondharrig to Xev. Fr. Dollard
in recognition of his efforts on behalf of, and his interest in,
“Gaelic pastimes.

Items.

At Wexford, Erin's Own (Waterford) defeated Sally
Beachers, 9-2 to 2-2, on Sunday, 13th April.

Congratulations have been received from the Waterford
S. P. and B. Association, New York, by the Waterford
‘I;{inlot hurling team on their success in the 1929 All-Ireland

nal,

In the first round of the Gaelic League tourney played
at Waterford, Erin’s Own defeated Tullaroan, 7-2 to 1-2,
on Sunday, April 27th.

Annual Congress.

The Annual Congress of the G.A.A. was held in the
Mansion House, Dublin, on Easter Sunday, 20th April,
with Mr. Sean Ryan in the chair. Mr. Ryan was unani-
mously elected President for the third year. The Chair-
man appealed for support for Irish industries, the language,
and dances. The general and provincial reports and finan-
cial statement, comprising the several accounts, were all
adopted. The playing of Rugby in Irish schools and colleges
was strongly criticised. A resolution was passed recom-
mending that all the work of the Association, whenever
possible, be transacted in Irish. It was decided that any
member of the Association who supplied notes, reports, or
information to British newspapers be expelled. The Con-
gress lasted nine hours and harmonious discussion character-
ised the proceedings.

“The Whitsuntide Tour,

The annual Whitsuntide tour to London and Liverpool
~ 'was continued this year, when Cork and Dublin filled the
.programme. The match at Liverpol was a draw, but Dub-
fin won the Ward Cup at London after a thrilling game at
Hearne Hill grounds.

The visitors were entertained at London by the local
‘County Board, and speeches were delivere by Messrs.
J. Collins, Chairman, and P. D. Breen, Wexiord, represent-
ing the Central Council. Mr. Owen Ward, the donor of the
wcup, gave a banquet to the visiting teams.

Mr. Phil O'Neill (“Sliabh Ruadh’’) accompanied the
'teams as special correspondent and wrote a lengthy deserip-
‘tive account of the tour for the ““Waterford Star.”

"The Munster Hurling Championships.

In the initial round Waterford defeated Limerick at
‘Waterford. Clare sprang a surprise by defeating Cork at
‘Limerick. In the semi-final the match between Tipperary
and Waterford at Dungarvan had to be abandoned owing to
-an altercation between the players. In the final Tipperary
scored a great victory over Clare in the Cork Afhletic
Grounds on Sunday, 27th July, and went on to win the
All-Treland from Dublin on the first Sunday in September.

"T.D., Galway, refereed. The Tipperary champions were
accorded a tremendous ovation when they arrived in
Thurles on Monday night, and Most Rev. Dr. Harty ad-

All-Ireland Finals and Semi-final,

On Sunday, 28th September, Kerry completely out-
- classed Monaghan in the football final. At no stage of the
game did the Northmen look like winners, and Kerr
romped home in traditional style, thus winning their nincf‘;
All-Ireland. Final scores:—Kerry, 8-4; Monaghan, 0-2.
In the Minor hurling final on the same date and venue
" Tipperary had a lucky win over Kilkenny, whose forwards
lost many golden opportunities. s
In the semi-final of the Junior All-Treland Hurling
* Championship Kilkenny defeated Antrim, who had pre-
viously played them to a drawn game at Belfast. Final
scores :—Kilkenny, 4-1; Antrim, 1-4. )

~Items,

At the Thurles Sports Field on Sunday, 5th October,
Toomevara and Boherlahan played a drawn game for the

" Tipperary Senior Hurling Championship final—5-5 to 6.2,

In the replay Toomevara had a decisive victory.

For the Wost Waterford football final, Dungarvan de-
feated Clashmore by 8 goals to 1 goal, on Sunday, 5th
October, at Dungarvan,

In the National Hurling League tie played at Water-

~ford, on Sunday, 12th October, Waterford defeated Lime-

rick, 4-3 to 8.2,

A “victory dance’” organised by the Cashel Gaels was
Tipperary champions who had re-
cently won the All-Treland final, on Sunday, 12th October.
Right Rev. Mgr. Ryan, P.P., V.G., presided at the banquet
and, referring to the dances that night, said he was sorry
to find that the dances staged on such occasions, were not

“purely Trish. A pleasant evening was spent.

Yor the final of #he Thomond Shield, Tipperary de-
feated Cork at Limerick on Sunday, 26th October, 6-5 to 3
goals. g

In the National Hurling League tie played at Kilkenny

~on 2nd November, Kilkenny defeated ‘Waterford, 4-5 to

1-2.
In the Natiomal Hurling League Cork defeated Water-

- “ford at Cork on Sunday, 16th November—6-3 to 2.1.

341

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

In the same competition played on the same }iate at
Limerick, Kilkenny defeated Limerick—3-4 to 8 points.

For the Waterford County football final, Dungsrvan-
defeated Rathgormack, 2-8 to 1-1, on Sunday, 20th Nov.

All-Ireland Junior Hurling Final.

Played at Waterford on Sunday, 28rd November, with
Mr. Sean Hogan as referee and before a crowd of 7,000
people, Tipperary decisively defeated Kilkenny on the scores
6-8 to 8-2. The gate receipts were £370.

Death of Dick Fitzgerald.

On Friday, 26th September, there passed away in
Killarney one of the greatest exponents of Gaelic football’
in the person of Dick Fitzgerald, who led the famous Kerry-
teams to victory on three or four occasions. ‘‘Dickeen,”
as he was more popularly known, was educated at St.
Brendan's College, Killarney, and later at Mungret College,
Limerick, where he became a close friend of the writer's.
He was prominent in the early Volunteer movement and!
was interned at Frongoch after the Easter Rising of 1016.
The following lines are a humble effort to pay a tribute te:
a great Gael and famous footballer. R.I.P.

IN MEMORIAM.

DICK FITZGERALD, OF KILLARNEY, WHO DIED
26th SEPTEMBER, 1930.

Over Loch Lein, where the sad moon pales,
Rises a caoine for a chief of the Gaels,

A leader gone from the fighting line—

A scion true of the Geraldine !

Through the mist of years his namo will gleam,
When he blazed the trail with his Kerry team,
And brought to the “Kingdom” name and fame
In the greatest tests of the Gaelic game;
Kildare and Wexford and Louth can tell

Of his deeds, and now his requiem swell,

And tribute pay to a gallant foe,

Who played the game as we Gaels know.

342

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

And when Ireland called when the fight was thick,
She called not in vain on our old friend Dick,

For he came with the boys who were never slack,
Who were led by Ashe and Austin Stack,

And he did his time on the cold plank bed,

When all thought Ireland’s cause was dead.

And now he’s gone from the field of fame,
But thousands still shall speak his name,
And tell of his deeds on the Gaelic field
For the Irish crown and the Railway Shield,

And whilst the Gacls the old games play,
And Kerry still may hold the sway,

We'll speak of him as a pioneer

Of the cause and the games we all hold dear,
And many a prayer will arise, T ween,

From the Gaels wno knew and loved “ Dickeen.”

—(Siapm Rvaon).

Tipperary’s Triple Crown.

By winning the three All-Trelands for 1030 in hurling
—Senior, Junior, and Minor—Tipperary achieved an
unique distinction in G.A.A, history. Rev. Father John
Meagher, Chairman of the Tipperary Co. Board, offered a
prize of £5 for the best poem on the subject, and the prize
was won by Mr. Tom Keating of Fethard. There were
several excellent attempts sent in, which have been pub-
lished in neat booklet form by the “‘Tipperary Star.” I
shall be pardoned for concluding my last chapter on the
History of the G.A.A. for Twenty Years by quoting Miss
Hayes’ (of Cashel) beautiful quatrain:—

“ It’s not a crown of diamonds,
Nor yet a crown of gold,
But a treble crown of laurels

That speaks of fame untold.”

(TP TO THE EASTER CONGRESS 1931)

Provincial Finals.

The provincial finals were played as usual on St.
Patrick’s Day, when, after two splendid games, the honours
went to Munster. In hurling, Munster won by a margin
of three points.

343

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A

The football contest lay between the individual Kerry
team and the pick of Leinster and after one of the finest
displays of the native code ever witnessed at headquarters
Kerry won by two points. Kerry, 2-2; Leinster, 6 points.

Changes in the Scores, Scoring Area, and in the Number of

Players.

From the foundation of the Association in 1834, and
from the start of the Championships in 1887, up to 1892,
the number of players was 21 aside, with a field 196 vards
in length.  No number of points equalled a goel.

In 1892 drastic changes were made in the rules. The
team that won the County Championship had the option of
selecting players from other clubs to nassist them m inter-
county and inter-provineial games. Five points were cqual
to a goal, and the number of players reduced from 21 to 17
aside. The scoring area was 7 yards between each pole, or
63 feet in all.

Tn 1901 the scoring area was reduced to 54 feet, or 6
yards between each pole, and later the area was raduced

355

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

further to 45 feet, or 5 yards between each pole. The
length of the ground during this period was 140 yards cr
not more than 180 yards.

At the Annual Congress of 1910 the scoring irea was
reduced from 15 yards to 7 yards, or 21 feet. The side
point posts were uﬂolished, and the goal posts extended two
yards. The length of the field to-day is usually 150 yards
—ten yards between the two 70 yards lines. This lengti,
however, may be extended to a maximum of 170 yards.

In 1913 the number of players was reduced from 17 to
15, which number is played to-day. The first big mateh
played with 15 aside was the final of the Croke Memoriad
football final between Kerry and Louth in May 1913,

In 1910 the parallelogram and the net behind the goais
were also introduced. The parallelogram extends four yards
beyond the goal posts, is 5 yards in depth, thus giving the
goalman an area of 15 yards by 5 yards.

Note.—Forrerr Pornts—Previous to 1892 forfeit points were
allowed, and five were equal to an ordinary pomt. A forfeit

int was allowed when one player of the defending side hit or
icked the ball over his own ?iue. After 1892 a fifty yards free
puck or kick was substituted. In 1910 the hurling puck was
extended from 50 to 70 yards.

I regret to state that it was impossible io get .the

Wexford's Third All-Ireland (1916).

Wexford won their third All-Treland on Sunday, 17th
December, 1916, when they defeated Mayo, Connacht cham-
pions, after a fast robust game. Half time scores: --Wex-
ford, 2-2; Mayo, 0-2. Full time:—Wexford, 3-4; Mayo,
1-2. Mr. Pat Dunphy (Leix) was referee, and the Wexiord

THE MUNSTER FINAL.
CORK v, TH’PER.—\RY,—D['NGARVAN,—A UGUST 1013.

I'was an Autumn day and the sun shone down
With a cheerful ray o'er the seaside town,

And dense the crowds that thronged them there
And joyous the shouts that rent the air;

For there were gathered our country’s pride
Erom Lee, Suir, Nore and Shannon side;

And came' they ‘ton from Sarsfield’s town

And forth from the shadow of Knockmealdown ::
E’en from the West were gathered there
Young men cpmely and maidens fair;

And with anxious step and eager pace,

Hurried they on to the trysting place,

For here to-day, in contest fleet,
Tipperary’s best and Cork will meet—
Here_beneath the Comeragh’s frown
They'll cross camins for the Munster crows.

Now, the hour's at hand and the crowds throng round,
Soon the hurlers invade the ground;
Deafening the cheers that greet these braves,
Each one the call of battle craves.
Tipperary decked in gold and biue,
evs of crimson hue;
urdy, lithe-limbed, well-set men,
out of arm and stern of ken,
Scions true of our ancient race,

And then in a flash that phalanx breaks!

Onward they press with might and main!
Onward, backward, on again!

Jiere they rush, there they meet,
Centremen, backmen, forwards fleet,

Then list to the click and creak and crash

is hero meets hero, ash to ash;

Now a bang, now a bound,

As the horseskin flitters across the ground :
Another moment it soars on high

Like a hunted bird through the Autumn sky:
ow like hare by greyhounds chased,

:ch the cry from every lip;

Thus for an hour the battle raged,

Those stalwarts hold, ‘unknowing fear,

CONCLUSION

Explanatory.

I lay down my pen with some diffidence, for now af
completion of my labour, I am quite aware how imj
and incomplete it is. Crities, no doubt, will have ple
pabulum to satisfy their pent-up appetites, and find
cient material to pillory Stiapa Ruaph on the altar of G
opinion. I ask them to be merciful, and to take s
mitigating circumstances into account. This was &
imposed task, and I was quite aware at the beginning
stupendous was the work before me. Yet, I tackle
the consciousness of doing something to perpetuate
G.A.A. in historical form, when abler pens than mine,
‘men in more favourable cireumstances as regards infor
tion and detail, had neglected it. This is my defence,
I am prepared to accept the verdict.

The project of writing a summary of G.A.A. activif
for the past twenty years was an idea of my own;
-when T broached the subject to Mr., W. J. Henebery of
KILKENNY JOURNAL, he not alone endorsed my sugges
‘but offered every assistance in his power to make
success.

During the course of compilation I had every recon s
40 the files of the KILKENNY JourNaL, from which 1 drew
latgely. Again, I was afforded the facility of examining the
files of the “‘Tipperary Star” and the ‘‘Munster Expross’
(Waterford), and I hereby tender sincere thanks to Mr.
Myles of the former and to Mr. Edward Walsh of the
latter. Both gentlemen were most courteous and obliging.

361

TWENTY YEARS OF THE G.A.A.

From the ‘“Tipperary Star™ I also received several excellent
blocks.

It will be noticed, as a result of my confined activities.
in the sphere of research, that there is a good deal of loeal
matter introduced, which I thought too good to reject.
Were I in a position to consult the files of the Metropolitam
or Cork Press, my work would be more detailed and
general and more reflective of Gaelic activity in the four
provinces. As it is, T have scarcely touched on Ulster or
Connacht for the reason I have already mentioned.

Thanks.

To the advertisers, who helped considerably to put this
bool on the market, T am under a deep obligation. I am
genuinely grateful for their support, and trust that the
Gaels in general will patronise their establishments. There
are three sections of Irish manufacturers who might have
availed of thig, work to bring their products before the

Gaels, and wify are continually harping on the apathy of

the Irish public’ towards native industries. I refer to the
Woollen Manufacturers; ‘‘the dark brothers’—the Boot
Manufacturers; and the Knitting Companies. This trio has
mich to gain by general Gaelic support, for their products
are bound-up largely with native games. The Tobacco.
Manufacturers, whose cigarettes are largely smoked by
Gaels, have lived true to their Imperial name and have
ignored every appeal to advertise their products in a pub-
lication of this kind. Biodh Acu!

To the staff of the Kmkenxy JourNar I desire also to
convey my thanks: to Mr. Henncbery, the Manager, and
to Mr. Canice Scully, who, in the setting up of the type
since the beginning, has imparted his largely-gained know-
ledge and experience to the perfect alignment of the subject
matter.

A Suggestion.

This book is merely an outline of general G.A.A. history
for the past twenty years. A comprehensive and dsalled
record , or series of records, is a work that must be under-
taken before long in the interests of the Irish peuple at
home and abroad. County Histories, at least, should be
undertaken by our school teachers or younger elergy, many
of whom have shed lustre on the Gaelic arena during their
student days. In this respect I am glad to learn that a
